## 1. First structural facts

For any \(d\mid 250\),
\[
|A_d\cap A_{250}|=\gcd(d,250)=d=|A_d|.
\]
So \(A_d\subseteq A_{250}\).

Also, setting \(i=j=d\), we get
\[
|A_d|=\gcd(d,d)=d.
\]
Hence \(A_{250}\) has exactly \(250\) elements, and every relevant \(A_d\) is a subset of this same \(250\)-element set.

Moreover, if \(e\mid f\), then
\[
|A_e\cap A_f|=e=|A_e|,
\]
so \(A_e\subseteq A_f\).

---

## 2. Decompose \(A_{250}\) into divisor-blocks

For each element \(x\in A_{250}\), consider the set of divisors \(d\mid 250\) such that \(x\in A_d\).  
Because intersections are governed by gcd and containment follows divisibility, this set has a unique least element \(m(x)\in D\), and then
\[
x\in A_d \iff m(x)\mid d.
\]

So if we define
\[
B_e=\{x\in A_{250}: m(x)=e\}\qquad (e\in D),
\]
then the \(B_e\) are disjoint and
\[
A_d=\bigcup_{e\mid d} B_e.
\]

Let \(b_e=|B_e|\). Then for each \(d\in D\),
\[
|A_d|=d=\sum_{e\mid d} b_e.
\]
By Möbius inversion,
\[
b_e=\varphi(e).
\]

Thus the block sizes are
\[
\varphi(1)=1,\quad \varphi(2)=1,\quad \varphi(5)=4,\quad \varphi(10)=4,
\]
\[
\varphi(25)=20,\quad \varphi(50)=20,\quad \varphi(125)=100,\quad \varphi(250)=100.
\]

---

## 3. Turn the problem into an ordering problem

To minimize maxima, we may relabel the \(250\) elements of \(A_{250}\) as \(1,2,\dots,250\) in increasing order.  
Only the relative order matters.

Since only the *last appearance* of each block \(B_e\) affects the maxima, we may place each block contiguously. So the problem becomes:

- arrange the 8 blocks \(B_e\),
- block \(B_e\) has length \(\varphi(e)\),
- for each \(d\in D\), \(\max A_d\) is the ending position of the latest block \(B_e\) with \(e\mid d\).

Now if \(e\mid f\) and block \(B_f\) is before \(B_e\), swapping them cannot increase any \(\max A_d\):
- any set containing \(f\) also contains \(e\),
- sets containing \(e\) but not \(f\) only improve.

So in an optimal arrangement, blocks respect divisibility:
\[
e\mid f \implies B_e \text{ appears before } B_f.
\]

Then for each \(d\in D\), all blocks \(B_e\) with \(e\mid d\) occur before \(B_d\), so
\[
\max A_d=\text{ending position of block }B_d.
\]

Thus we now just need to minimize the sum of completion times of the 8 jobs \(B_d\), with:
- processing times \(p_d=\varphi(d)\),
- precedence \(e\prec f\) whenever \(e\mid f\).

For adjacent incomparable jobs, shorter first is better, so we use shortest-processing-time among currently available jobs.

This gives the order
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

Their lengths are
\[
1,\ 1,\ 4,\ 4,\ 20,\ 20,\ 100,\ 100.
\]

So the completion times are
\[
1,\ 2,\ 6,\ 10,\ 30,\ 50,\ 150,\ 250.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250
=499.
\]

---

## 4. Achievability

Take disjoint blocks:
\[
B_1=\{1\},\quad B_2=\{2\},\quad B_5=\{3,4,5,6\},\quad B_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\},
\]
\[
B_{25}=\{11,\dots,30\},\quad B_{50}=\{31,\dots,50\},
\]
\[
B_{125}=\{51,\dots,150\},\quad B_{250}=\{151,\dots,250\}.
\]

Define
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n} B_d
\]
(for all \(n\), adding disjoint new blocks \(B_d\) for divisors \(d\nmid 250\) if needed).

Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(d)=\gcd(i,j),
\]
and the above maxima are exactly the completion times listed.

So \(499\) is attainable.

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let  
\[
S=\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d.
\]
We want the minimum possible value of \(S\).

## 1. Structural form of the sets

Since
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j),
\]
taking \(i=j\) gives
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Also, if \(i\mid j\), then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=i=|A_i|,
\]
so \(A_i\subseteq A_j\).

Now for any element \(x\), let
\[
M_x=\{n:x\in A_n\}.
\]
Because \(A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}\), if \(x\in A_i\) and \(x\in A_j\), then \(x\in A_{\gcd(i,j)}\). Thus \(M_x\) is closed under gcd. Since it is also upward-closed under divisibility, \(M_x\) must be exactly the set of multiples of some positive integer \(m\).

Let \(X_m\) be the set of elements whose “minimal index” is \(m\). Then the \(X_m\) are disjoint and
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{m\mid n} X_m.
\]
Let \(c_m=|X_m|\). Then
\[
|A_n|=\sum_{m\mid n} c_m=n.
\]
By Möbius inversion (or the identity \(\sum_{d\mid n}\varphi(d)=n\)),
\[
c_m=\varphi(m).
\]

So necessarily,
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{m\mid n} X_m,\qquad |X_m|=\varphi(m).
\]

---

## 2. Only divisors of \(250\) matter

The divisors of \(250=2\cdot 5^3\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]
For these,
\[
A_d=\bigsqcup_{m\mid d} X_m,
\]
so only the blocks \(X_m\) with \(m\mid 250\) affect the sum.

Their sizes are:
\[
\varphi(1)=1,\ \varphi(2)=1,\ \varphi(5)=4,\ \varphi(10)=4,\ \varphi(25)=20,\ \varphi(50)=20,\ \varphi(125)=100,\ \varphi(250)=100.
\]
These sum to
\[
1+1+4+4+20+20+100+100=250=|A_{250}|.
\]

Since \(\max A_{250}\ge 250\), in a minimum configuration we may label the elements of \(A_{250}\) by \(1,2,\dots,250\), and all other elements larger. So we only need to order these eight blocks.

---

## 3. Reduce to ordering blocks

Think of each block \(X_m\) as a job of length \(\varphi(m)\). Put the blocks in some order, contiguously. Then the completion time \(C_m\) of \(X_m\) is the largest label in that block.

For each divisor \(d\mid 250\),
\[
A_d=\bigsqcup_{m\mid d} X_m,
\]
so
\[
\max A_d=\max_{m\mid d} C_m.
\]

### Key swap 1: divisibility order
If \(x\mid y\) but \(X_y\) is placed before \(X_x\), swapping them cannot increase any \(\max A_d\):

- if \(y\mid d\), then also \(x\mid d\), so \(A_d\) contains both blocks, and the later of the two still finishes after both are done;
- if \(x\mid d\) but \(y\nmid d\), then moving \(X_x\) earlier only helps.

Thus we may assume:
\[
x\mid y \implies X_x \text{ is before } X_y.
\]

Under this order, for each \(d\mid 250\), all proper divisor-blocks of \(d\) come before \(X_d\), so
\[
\max A_d=C_d.
\]
Therefore
\[
S=\sum_{d\mid 250} C_d.
\]

So now we just need a linear extension of the divisibility poset minimizing the sum of completion times.

---

## 4. Optimal topological order

Among two adjacent incomparable blocks, putting the shorter one first decreases the sum of completion times.

So we repeatedly choose the shortest available block.

The precedence relations are by divisibility. The available choices go as follows:

- First \(1\).
- Then available: \(2,5\) → choose \(2\) (sizes \(1,4\)).
- Then only \(5\).
- Then available: \(10,25\) → choose \(10\) (sizes \(4,20\)).
- Then \(25\).
- Then available: \(50,125\) → choose \(50\) (sizes \(20,100\)).
- Then \(125\).
- Then \(250\).

Thus the optimal order is
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

Now compute cumulative sums:

\[
\begin{aligned}
C_1&=1,\\
C_2&=1+1=2,\\
C_5&=2+4=6,\\
C_{10}&=6+4=10,\\
C_{25}&=10+20=30,\\
C_{50}&=30+20=50,\\
C_{125}&=50+100=150,\\
C_{250}&=150+100=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
S=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

---

## 5. Achievability

Take disjoint blocks:
- \(X_1=\{1\}\),
- \(X_2=\{2\}\),
- \(X_5=\{3,4,5,6\}\),
- \(X_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\}\),
- \(X_{25}=\{11,\dots,30\}\),
- \(X_{50}=\{31,\dots,50\}\),
- \(X_{125}=\{51,\dots,150\}\),
- \(X_{250}=\{151,\dots,250\}\),

and for all other \(m\), choose disjoint sets \(X_m\) of size \(\varphi(m)\) using integers \(>250\). Define
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{m\mid n} X_m.
\]
Then \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\), and the above maxima are attained.

Therefore the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
D=\{1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250\},
\]
and for \(d\in D\), write
\[
m_d=\max A_d.
\]

We want the minimum of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d.
\]

## 1. A useful structural fact
Since \(|A_i|=|A_i\cap A_i|=\gcd(i,i)=i\), we have
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Also, if \(g=\gcd(i,j)\), then \(g\mid i\) and \(g\mid j\), so
\[
A_g\subseteq A_i\cap A_j
\]
because if \(g\mid i\), then
\[
|A_g\cap A_i|=\gcd(g,i)=g=|A_g|,
\]
hence \(A_g\subseteq A_i\). Similarly \(A_g\subseteq A_j\).

But both \(A_g\) and \(A_i\cap A_j\) have size \(g\), so actually
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

Therefore,
\[
|A_i\cup A_j|=|A_i|+|A_j|-|A_i\cap A_j|
=i+j-\gcd(i,j).
\]

In particular,
\[
|A_2\cup A_5|=2+5-1=6,
\]
\[
|A_{10}\cup A_{25}|=10+25-5=30,
\]
\[
|A_{50}\cup A_{125}|=50+125-25=150.
\]

## 2. Lower bound
Sort the eight numbers \(m_d\) increasingly:
\[
c_1\le c_2\le \cdots \le c_8.
\]

We now bound each \(c_k\):

- \(c_1\ge 1\), since every set is nonempty.
- \(c_2\ge 2\), since only \(A_1\) can have maximum \(1\) (all other sets have at least 2 elements).
- \(c_3\ge 6\): if \(c_3\le 5\), then three sets would lie inside \(\{1,2,3,4,5\}\). The only possible candidates are \(A_1,A_2,A_5\), but then \(A_2\cup A_5\) would have to fit in 5 numbers, contradicting \(|A_2\cup A_5|=6\).
- \(c_4\ge 10\), since any fourth set among these has at least 10 elements.
- \(c_5\ge 30\): if \(c_5\le 29\), then \(A_1,A_2,A_5,A_{10},A_{25}\) would all lie in \(\{1,\dots,29\}\), but
  \[
  |A_{10}\cup A_{25}|=30,
  \]
  impossible.
- \(c_6\ge 50\), since the sixth-smallest maximum must come from a set of size at least 50.
- \(c_7\ge 150\): if \(c_7\le 149\), then \(A_1,A_2,A_5,A_{10},A_{25},A_{50},A_{125}\) would all lie in \(\{1,\dots,149\}\), but
  \[
  |A_{50}\cup A_{125}|=150,
  \]
  impossible.
- \(c_8\ge 250\), since \(|A_{250}|=250\).

So
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d
=\sum_{k=1}^8 c_k
\ge 1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

## 3. Construction achieving 499
Define disjoint blocks \(B_n\) with \(|B_n|=\varphi(n)\), and set
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n} B_d.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|
=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(d)
=\gcd(i,j),
\]
using \(\sum_{d\mid m}\varphi(d)=m\).

For the divisors of \(250\), choose:
\[
B_1=\{1\},\quad
B_2=\{2\},\quad
B_5=\{3,4,5,6\},
\]
\[
B_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\},\quad
B_{25}=\{11,\dots,30\},
\]
\[
B_{50}=\{31,\dots,50\},\quad
B_{125}=\{51,\dots,150\},
\]
\[
B_{250}=\{151,\dots,250\}.
\]
(For all other \(n\), place \(B_n\) disjointly using integers \(>250\).)

Then:
\[
\max A_1=1,\quad
\max A_2=2,\quad
\max A_5=6,\quad
\max A_{10}=10,
\]
\[
\max A_{25}=30,\quad
\max A_{50}=50,\quad
\max A_{125}=150,\quad
\max A_{250}=250.
\]
Hence
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

Therefore the minimum possible value is
\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let the centers of the three small circles be \(A,B,C\). Since each small circle has radius \(2\) and is internally tangent to \(\Omega\) of radius \(11\), we have

\[
OA=OB=OC=11-2=9.
\]

So \(A,B,C\) lie on the circle of radius \(9\) centered at \(O\).

---

### 1. Relate a chord length to the angle between two small-circle centers
Consider the chord tangent to the circles centered at \(B\) and \(C\).  
Because the two circles have equal radii, their common tangent inside \(\Omega\) is parallel to \(BC\).

Let
\[
\angle BOC=\theta.
\]
Then the distance from \(O\) to the line \(BC\) is
\[
9\cos\frac{\theta}{2}.
\]
Since the tangent chord is \(2\) units closer to \(O\) than \(BC\), its distance from \(O\) is

\[
d=9\cos\frac{\theta}{2}-2.
\]

If a chord of a circle of radius \(11\) is at distance \(d\) from the center, then its length is

\[
L=2\sqrt{11^2-d^2}=2\sqrt{121-d^2}.
\]

---

### 2. Use the given chord length
Two chords have length \(4\sqrt{30}\). So for each of those chords,

\[
2\sqrt{121-d^2}=4\sqrt{30}.
\]

Thus

\[
\sqrt{121-d^2}=2\sqrt{30}
\]
\[
121-d^2=120
\]
\[
d^2=1.
\]

Since \(O\) lies inside the triangle formed by the chords, the distance is positive, so

\[
d=1.
\]

Therefore for each of those two chords,

\[
9\cos\frac{\theta}{2}-2=1
\]
\[
9\cos\frac{\theta}{2}=3
\]
\[
\cos\frac{\theta}{2}=\frac13.
\]

So the two corresponding central angles each satisfy

\[
\frac{\theta}{2}=\arccos\frac13.
\]

---

### 3. Find the third central angle
Let the three central angles between consecutive centers be \(\alpha,\beta,\gamma\), with two of them corresponding to the given equal chords. Then

\[
\alpha=\beta=2\arccos\frac13,
\]
and since they go all the way around the center,

\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=2\pi.
\]

Hence

\[
\frac{\gamma}{2}
= \pi-\arccos\frac13-\arccos\frac13
= \pi-2\arccos\frac13.
\]

Now compute:

\[
\cos\frac{\gamma}{2}
= \cos\!\left(\pi-2\arccos\frac13\right)
= -\cos\!\left(2\arccos\frac13\right).
\]

Using \(\cos 2x=2\cos^2x-1\),

\[
\cos\!\left(2\arccos\frac13\right)
=2\left(\frac13\right)^2-1
=\frac29-1
=-\frac79.
\]

So

\[
\cos\frac{\gamma}{2}=\frac79.
\]

Thus the distance from \(O\) to the third chord is

\[
d_3=9\cdot\frac79-2=7-2=5.
\]

---

### 4. Compute the third chord length
Now

\[
L_3=2\sqrt{121-5^2}
=2\sqrt{121-25}
=2\sqrt{96}
=8\sqrt6.
\]

\[
\boxed{8\sqrt6}
\]

\[
OA=OB=OC=11-2=9.

So \(A,B,C\) all lie on the circle of radius \(9\) centered at \(O\).

## 1. Convert chord length to distance from \(O\)

If a chord of \(\Omega\) has distance \(d\) from \(O\), then its length is

\[
L=2\sqrt{11^2-d^2}=2\sqrt{121-d^2}.
\]

Given two chords have length \(4\sqrt{30}\),

\[
2\sqrt{121-d^2}=4\sqrt{30}

\[

\[

\]

\[

## 2. Relate this distance to the angle between two circle centers

Consider one such chord tangent to the two small circles centered at \(A\) and \(B\). Let

\[
\angle AOB=\theta.
\]

Because the chord is tangent to both small circles, the perpendicular distance from \(A\) and \(B\) to the chord is \(2\). Since the chord is \(1\) unit from \(O\), the projection of \(OA\) (and \(OB\)) onto the chord’s normal is

\[
1+2=3.
\]

But that same projection equals

\[
9\cos\frac{\theta}{2}.
\]

Thus

\[
9\cos\frac{\theta}{2}=3
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\cos\frac{\theta}{2}=\frac13.
\]

So

\[
\cos\theta=2\left(\frac13\right)^2-1=\frac{2}{9}-1=-\frac79.
\]

---

## 3. Determine the third central angle

Two chords have the same length, so two of the pairwise angles among \(A,B,C\) are equal to \(\theta\). The three centers lie on the circle around \(O\), so the third pairwise minor angle is

\[
\phi=2\pi-2\theta.
\]

Then

\[
\cos\frac{\phi}{2}
=\cos(\pi-\theta)
=-\cos\theta
=\frac79.
\]

---

## 4. Find the distance of the third chord from \(O\)

For the third chord, tangent to the remaining pair of circles, its distance \(d_3\) from \(O\) satisfies

\[
d_3+2=9\cos\frac{\phi}{2}=9\cdot\frac79=7.
\]

Hence

\[
d_3=5.
\]

Its length is therefore

\[
L_3=2\sqrt{121-5^2}
=2\sqrt{121-25}
=2\sqrt{96}
=8\sqrt6.
\]

\[

### 2. Put the two equal chords symmetrically
Place \(O=(0,0)\), and let the two equal chords be symmetric about the \(x\)-axis:
\[
\ell_1:\cos\theta\,x+\sin\theta\,y=1,\qquad
\ell_2:\cos\theta\,x-\sin\theta\,y=1.

The small circle tangent to both \(\ell_1\) and \(\ell_2\) has radius \(2\), so its center lies on the parallel lines
\[

\]
Their intersection is
\[
\left(\frac{3}{\cos\theta},\,0\right).
\]
Since each small circle is internally tangent to \(\Omega\), its center is \(11-2=9\) units from \(O\). Thus
\[
\frac{3}{\cos\theta}=9 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \cos\theta=\frac13.
\]
Hence
\[
\sin\theta=\sqrt{1-\frac19}=\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}.
\]

Multiplying the line equations by \(3\), we get
\[
\ell_1: x+2\sqrt2\,y=3,\qquad
\ell_2: x-2\sqrt2\,y=3.
\]

---

### 3. Find the other two small-circle centers
By symmetry, the third chord is vertical, and the other two small-circle centers are reflections across the \(x\)-axis.

A center tangent to \(\ell_1\) must lie on the line parallel to \(\ell_1\) at distance \(2\), namely
\[
x+2\sqrt2\,y=9.
\]
It also lies on the circle of radius \(9\) centered at \(O\):
\[
x^2+y^2=81.
\]

Substitute \(y=\dfrac{9-x}{2\sqrt2}\):
\[
x^2+\left(\frac{9-x}{2\sqrt2}\right)^2=81.
\]
This simplifies to
\[
9x^2-18x-567=0
\]
\[
x^2-2x-63=0
\]
\[
(x-9)(x+7)=0.
\]
So \(x=9\) or \(x=-7\).

The point \(x=9\) is the center of the first small circle, so the other center is
\[
(-7,\,4\sqrt2).
\]
Its reflection is
\[
(-7,\,-4\sqrt2).
\]

Thus the third chord is tangent to these two circles, so it is the vertical line \(2\) units to the right of their centers:
\[
x=-5.
\]

So the distance from \(O\) to the third chord is \(5\).

---

### 4. Compute the third chord length
Now
\[
L=2\sqrt{11^2-5^2}=2\sqrt{121-25}=2\sqrt{96}=4\sqrt6.
\]

\[
\boxed{4\sqrt6}
\]

Let the three small circles have centers \(A,B,C\). Since each small circle has radius \(2\) and is internally tangent to \(\Omega\) of radius \(11\), we have

\[
OA=OB=OC=11-2=9.
\]

So \(A,B,C\) lie on the circle centered at \(O\) with radius \(9\).

---

### 1. Relating a chord to the angle between two small-circle centers
Consider the chord of \(\Omega\) tangent to the circles centered at \(B\) and \(C\).

Because the two small circles have equal radii, that tangent line is parallel to \(\overline{BC}\).  
Let

\[
\theta = \angle BOC.
\]

Since \(B,C\) lie on the circle of radius \(9\), the distance from \(O\) to the line \(BC\) is

\[
9\cos\frac{\theta}{2}.
\]

The tangent chord is \(2\) units closer to \(O\) than \(BC\), so its distance from \(O\) is

\[
d = 9\cos\frac{\theta}{2} - 2.
\]

If a chord of \(\Omega\) is at distance \(d\) from the center of \(\Omega\), then its length is

\[
L=2\sqrt{11^2-d^2}=2\sqrt{121-d^2}.
\]

---

### 2. Use the given chord length
Two chords have length \(4\sqrt{30}\). So for each of those chords,

\[
2\sqrt{121-d^2}=4\sqrt{30}.
\]

Divide by \(2\):

\[
\sqrt{121-d^2}=2\sqrt{30}.
\]

Square:

\[
121-d^2=120 \quad\Rightarrow\quad d^2=1.
\]

Since \(O\) lies inside the triangle, the relevant distance is \(d=1\). Thus

\[
9\cos\frac{\theta}{2}-2=1
\]

so

\[
9\cos\frac{\theta}{2}=3
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\cos\frac{\theta}{2}=\frac13.
\]

So the two corresponding central angles each satisfy

\[
\cos\frac{\theta}{2}=\frac13.
\]

---

### 3. Find the third angle
Let the three central angles between consecutive centers be \(\alpha,\beta,\gamma\). Then

\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=360^\circ.
\]

Two of the chords have length \(4\sqrt{30}\), so for two of these angles,

\[
\cos\frac{\alpha}{2}=\cos\frac{\beta}{2}=\frac13.
\]

Let

\[
x=\frac{\alpha}{2}=\frac{\beta}{2},
\quad\text{so}\quad \cos x=\frac13.
\]

Then

\[
\frac{\gamma}{2}=180^\circ-x-x=180^\circ-2x.
\]

Hence

\[
\cos\frac{\gamma}{2}=\cos(180^\circ-2x)=-\cos 2x.
\]

Now

\[
\cos 2x = 2\cos^2 x-1 = 2\left(\frac13\right)^2-1=\frac{2}{9}-1=-\frac79.
\]

Therefore

\[
\cos\frac{\gamma}{2} = -\left(-\frac79\right)=\frac79.
\]

---

### 4. Distance of the third chord from \(O\)
For the third chord,

\[
d_3=9\cos\frac{\gamma}{2}-2
=9\left(\frac79\right)-2
=7-2
=5.
\]

So its length is

\[
L_3=2\sqrt{121-5^2}
=2\sqrt{121-25}
=2\sqrt{96}
=8\sqrt6.
\]

\[
\boxed{8\sqrt6}
\]

Let the centers of the three small circles be \(A,B,C\). Since each small circle has radius \(2\) and is internally tangent to \(\Omega\) of radius \(11\), we have

\[
OA=OB=OC=11-2=9.
\]

So \(A,B,C\) lie on the circle centered at \(O\) with radius \(9\).

Now consider the chord of \(\Omega\) tangent to the circles centered at \(A\) and \(B\). Let that chord lie on line \(\ell\), and let

\[
\angle AOB=\theta.
\]

Because \(\ell\) is tangent to both equal circles, the perpendicular distances from \(A\) and \(B\) to \(\ell\) are both \(2\), so \(\ell\) is parallel to \(AB\).

The distance from \(O\) to the line \(AB\) is the standard chord-distance formula in the radius-\(9\) circle:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(O,AB)=9\cos\frac{\theta}{2}.
\]

Since \(\ell\) is parallel to \(AB\) and \(2\) units closer to \(O\), the distance from \(O\) to \(\ell\) is

\[
d=9\cos\frac{\theta}{2}-2.
\]

---

### Use the given chord length
A chord of \(\Omega\) with distance \(d\) from the center has length

\[
L=2\sqrt{11^2-d^2}.
\]

Given two chords have length \(4\sqrt{30}\), for each of those chords:

\[
4\sqrt{30}=2\sqrt{121-d^2}.
\]

So

\[
2\sqrt{30}=\sqrt{121-d^2}
\]
\[
120=121-d^2
\]
\[
d^2=1.
\]

Since \(O\) lies inside the triangle formed by the chords, \(d=1\). Thus

\[
9\cos\frac{\theta}{2}-2=1
\]
\[
9\cos\frac{\theta}{2}=3
\]
\[
\cos\frac{\theta}{2}=\frac13.
\]

So the two corresponding central angles are equal; let each be \(\theta\).

---

### Find the third central angle
The three central angles between \(A,B,C\) sum to \(360^\circ\), so if two are \(\theta\), the third is

\[
360^\circ-2\theta.
\]

Let the third angle be \(\phi\). Then

\[
\phi=360^\circ-2\theta.
\]

Hence

\[
\frac{\phi}{2}=180^\circ-\theta.
\]

We know

\[
\cos\theta=2\cos^2\frac{\theta}{2}-1
=2\left(\frac13\right)^2-1
=\frac{2}{9}-1
=-\frac79.
\]

Therefore

\[
\cos\frac{\phi}{2}=\cos(180^\circ-\theta)=-\cos\theta=\frac79.
\]

So the distance from \(O\) to the third chord is

\[
d_3=9\cos\frac{\phi}{2}-2
=9\cdot\frac79-2
=7-2=5.
\]

Thus its length is

\[
L_3=2\sqrt{11^2-5^2}
=2\sqrt{121-25}
=2\sqrt{96}
=8\sqrt6.
\]

\[
\boxed{8\sqrt6}
\]

Let the centers of the three small circles be \(A,B,C\). Since each small circle has radius \(2\) and is internally tangent to \(\Omega\) of radius \(11\), we have

\[
OA=OB=OC=11-2=9.
\]

So \(A,B,C\) lie on the circle centered at \(O\) with radius \(9\).

## 1. Relate a chord of \(\Omega\) to the segment joining two small-circle centers
Each chord of \(\Omega\) is tangent to two of the small circles.  
For example, suppose one chord is tangent to the circles centered at \(B\) and \(C\).

Because the two circles have equal radius, that chord is parallel to \(BC\). Also, since the chord is tangent to each small circle, the distance between the chord and line \(BC\) is \(2\).

Since \(O\) lies inside the triangle formed by the three chords, \(O\) is on the opposite side of the chord from the centers, so

\[
\text{dist}(O,BC)=\text{dist}(O,\text{chord})+2.
\]

## 2. Use the given chord length \(4\sqrt{30}\)
For a chord of \(\Omega\) of length \(4\sqrt{30}\), if its distance from \(O\) is \(d\), then

\[
2\sqrt{11^2-d^2}=4\sqrt{30}.
\]

So

\[
\sqrt{121-d^2}=2\sqrt{30}
\]
\[
121-d^2=120
\]
\[
d=1.
\]

Thus for each of the two given chords,

\[
\text{dist}(O,BC)=1+2=3.
\]

## 3. Convert this to a central angle among \(A,B,C\)
Since \(B,C\) lie on the circle of radius \(9\), the distance from \(O\) to chord \(BC\) is

\[
9\cos\left(\frac{\angle BOC}{2}\right)=3.
\]

Hence

\[
\cos\left(\frac{\angle BOC}{2}\right)=\frac13.
\]

Let

\[
\theta=\angle BOC=\angle COA.
\]

Then

\[
\cos\left(\frac\theta2\right)=\frac13.
\]

The third central angle is

\[
\angle AOB=2\pi-2\theta.
\]

Let this angle be \(\phi\). Then

\[
\phi=2\pi-2\theta.
\]

So

\[
\frac{\phi}{2}=\pi-\theta.
\]

Now compute:

\[
\cos\theta = 2\cos^2\left(\frac\theta2\right)-1
=2\left(\frac13\right)^2-1
=\frac29-1
=-\frac79.
\]

Therefore

\[
\cos\left(\frac{\phi}{2}\right)=\cos(\pi-\theta)=-\cos\theta=\frac79.
\]

Thus the distance from \(O\) to line \(AB\) is

\[
9\cos\left(\frac{\phi}{2}\right)=9\cdot \frac79=7.
\]

So the distance from \(O\) to the third tangent chord is

\[
7-2=5.
\]

## 4. Find the third chord length
A chord of \(\Omega\) at distance \(5\) from \(O\) has length

\[
2\sqrt{11^2-5^2}
=2\sqrt{121-25}
=2\sqrt{96}
=8\sqrt6.
\]

\[
\boxed{8\sqrt6}
\]

Let  
\[
\angle BAC=2\alpha,
\]
so the given conditions are
\[
\angle PMB=\angle QMC=\alpha.
\]

We want \(BP\). If \(AB=b\) and \(AC=c\), then since \(AP=1\),
\[
BP=b-1.
\]

So it remains to find \(b\).

---

## 1) Put the triangle in coordinates

Place \(A=(0,0)\), and put the angle bisector of \(\angle A\) on the \(x\)-axis. Then we may write
\[
B=(b\cos\alpha,\; b\sin\alpha),\qquad
C=(c\cos\alpha,\; -c\sin\alpha).
\]

Then:

- \(AB=b\),
- \(AC=c\),
- \(BC=8\).

Also,
\[
P=(\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha),\qquad
Q=(3\cos\alpha,-3\sin\alpha).
\]

Since \(M\) is the midpoint of \(BC\),
\[
M=\left(\frac{(b+c)\cos\alpha}{2},\;\frac{(b-c)\sin\alpha}{2}\right).
\]

---

## 2) Use the condition \(\angle PMB=\alpha\)

Compute the vectors:
\[
\overrightarrow{MB}
=\left(\frac{(b-c)\cos\alpha}{2},\;\frac{(b+c)\sin\alpha}{2}\right),
\]
\[
\overrightarrow{MP}
=\left(\frac{(2-b-c)\cos\alpha}{2},\;\frac{(2-b+c)\sin\alpha}{2}\right).
\]

Because the angle between these vectors is \(\alpha\),
\[
\tan\alpha
=\frac{|\overrightarrow{MB}\times \overrightarrow{MP}|}{\overrightarrow{MB}\cdot\overrightarrow{MP}}.
\]

Carrying out the determinant and dot product simplification gives
\[
(b-1)(b+c)=32. \tag{1}
\]

---

## 3) Use the condition \(\angle QMC=\alpha\)

By the same computation on the \(C\)-side (using \(AQ=3\)), we get
\[
(c-3)(b+c)=32. \tag{2}
\]

---

## 4) Solve for \(b\) and \(c\)

From (1) and (2),
\[
(b-1)(b+c)=(c-3)(b+c).
\]
Since \(b+c>0\), we have
\[
b-1=c-3 \quad\Rightarrow\quad c=b+2.
\]

Substitute into (1):
\[
(b-1)(b+c)=(b-1)(2b+2)=32.
\]
So
\[
2(b-1)(b+1)=32
\]
\[
(b-1)(b+1)=16
\]
\[
b^2-1=16
\]
\[
b^2=17.
\]
Thus
\[
b=\sqrt{17}.
\]

Therefore
\[
BP=AB-AP=b-1=\sqrt{17}-1.
\]

\[
\boxed{\sqrt{17}-1}
\]

Let  
\[
x=BP,\qquad y=CQ.
\]
Then
\[
AB=AP+PB=1+x,\qquad AC=AQ+CQ=3+y.
\]

We place the triangle in coordinates:
\[
M=(0,0),\quad B=(-4,0),\quad C=(4,0),\quad A=(d,h)\quad (h>0),
\]
since \(BC=8\) and \(M\) is the midpoint of \(BC\).

## 1) Coordinates of \(P\) and \(Q\)

Because \(P\) divides \(\overline{AB}\) with \(AP:PB=1:x\),
\[
P=\left(\frac{xd-4}{x+1},\frac{xh}{x+1}\right).
\]

Because \(Q\) divides \(\overline{AC}\) with \(AQ:CQ=3:y\),
\[
Q=\left(\frac{yd+12}{y+3},\frac{yh}{y+3}\right).
\]

Let
\[
\alpha=\frac12\angle BAC.
\]
Given
\[
\angle PMB=\alpha,\qquad \angle QMC=\alpha.
\]
Since \(MB\) points along the negative \(x\)-axis and \(MC\) along the positive \(x\)-axis,
\[
\tan\alpha=\frac{P_y}{-P_x}=\frac{xh}{4-xd},
\qquad
\tan\alpha=\frac{Q_y}{Q_x}=\frac{yh}{yd+12}.
\]
Let \(t=\tan\alpha\). Then
\[
t=\frac{xh}{4-xd}=\frac{yh}{yd+12}.
\]

From these,
\[
h/t=\frac4x-d,\qquad h/t=d+\frac{12}{y}.
\]
So
\[
d=\frac2x-\frac6y,
\qquad
\frac ht=\frac2x+\frac6y.
\tag{1}
\]

---

## 2) Use the half-angle formula

In \(\triangle ABC\), let
\[
a=BC=8,\quad b=CA=3+y,\quad c=AB=1+x.
\]
Also the area is
\[
[ABC]=\frac12\cdot 8\cdot h=4h.
\]
Using
\[
\tan\frac A2=\frac{4[ABC]}{(b+c)^2-a^2},
\]
we get
\[
t=\frac{16h}{(x+y+4)^2-64}.
\]
Thus
\[
\frac ht=\frac{(x+y+4)^2-64}{16}.
\]
Combine with (1):
\[
\frac2x+\frac6y=\frac{(x+y+4)^2-64}{16}.
\]
Multiply by \(16\):
\[
\frac{32}{x}+\frac{96}{y}=(x+y+4)^2-64.
\tag{2}
\]

---

## 3) Use \(AB^2-AC^2\)

From coordinates,
\[
AB^2=(d+4)^2+h^2,\qquad AC^2=(d-4)^2+h^2,
\]
so
\[
AB^2-AC^2=16d.
\]
But also
\[
AB=1+x,\qquad AC=3+y,
\]
hence
\[
(1+x)^2-(3+y)^2=16d.
\]
Using \(d=\frac2x-\frac6y\) from (1),
\[
(1+x)^2-(3+y)^2=\frac{32}{x}-\frac{96}{y}.
\tag{3}
\]

---

## 4) Solve for \(x,y\)

Add (2) and (3):
\[
\frac{64}{x}
=
\big((x+y+4)^2-64\big)+\big((1+x)^2-(3+y)^2\big).
\]
The right side simplifies to
\[
2\big((x+1)(x+y+4)-32\big).
\]
So
\[
\frac{32}{x}=(x+1)(x+y+4)-32.
\]
Multiply by \(x\):
\[
32=x(x+1)(x+y+4)-32x.
\]
Rearrange:
\[
(x+1)\big(x(x+y+4)-32\big)=0.
\]
Since \(x+1\neq0\),
\[
x(x+y+4)=32.
\tag{4}
\]

Now subtract (3) from (2):
\[
\frac{192}{y}
=
\big((x+y+4)^2-64\big)-\big((1+x)^2-(3+y)^2\big),
\]
which simplifies to
\[
2\big((y+3)(x+y+4)-32\big).
\]
Thus
\[
\frac{96}{y}=(y+3)(x+y+4)-32,
\]
and similarly,
\[
y(x+y+4)=32.
\tag{5}
\]

From (4) and (5),
\[
x(x+y+4)=y(x+y+4).
\]
Since \(x+y+4>0\), we get
\[
x=y.
\]

Plug into (4):
\[
x(2x+4)=32
\]
\[
x^2+2x-16=0.
\]
So
\[
x=-1\pm\sqrt{17}.
\]
Since \(x=BP>0\),
\[
x=\sqrt{17}-1.
\]

Therefore,
\[
\boxed{\sqrt{17}-1}.
\]

Let  
\[
\angle BAC=2\alpha,\qquad x=BP.
\]

Then
\[
AB=AP+PB=1+x,\qquad AC=AQ+CQ=3+CQ.
\]

We first show \(CQ=x\).

### 1) Compute \(BP\) and \(CQ\) with the Law of Sines
In \(\triangle BMP\), we have
\[
BM=4,\qquad \angle PMB=\alpha,\qquad \angle MBP=\angle ABC=B.
\]
So
\[
\angle BPM=180^\circ-(B+\alpha),
\]
and by the Law of Sines,
\[
\frac{x}{\sin\alpha}=\frac{4}{\sin(B+\alpha)}.
\]
Hence
\[
x=\frac{4\sin\alpha}{\sin(B+\alpha)}. \tag{1}
\]

Similarly, in \(\triangle CMQ\),
\[
CM=4,\qquad \angle QMC=\alpha,\qquad \angle MCQ=\angle ACB=C,
\]
so
\[
CQ=\frac{4\sin\alpha}{\sin(C+\alpha)}. \tag{2}
\]

But in \(\triangle ABC\),
\[
B+C+2\alpha=180^\circ \implies (B+\alpha)+(C+\alpha)=180^\circ,
\]
so
\[
\sin(B+\alpha)=\sin(C+\alpha).
\]
Thus from (1) and (2),
\[
CQ=x.
\]

Therefore
\[
AC=AQ+CQ=3+x.
\]

So the side lengths are
\[
AB=x+1,\qquad AC=x+3,\qquad BC=8.
\]

---

### 2) Use the Law of Sines in \(\triangle ABC\)
From \(\triangle ABC\),
\[
AB=\frac{8\sin C}{\sin 2\alpha}=x+1,\qquad AC=\frac{8\sin B}{\sin 2\alpha}=x+3. \tag{3}
\]

Let
\[
d=\frac{B-C}{2}.
\]
Since
\[
B+C=180^\circ-2\alpha,
\]
we get
\[
B=90^\circ-\alpha+d,\qquad C=90^\circ-\alpha-d.
\]

Subtract the two equations in (3):
\[
2=\frac{8(\sin B-\sin C)}{\sin 2\alpha}.
\]
Using
\[
\sin B-\sin C=2\cos\frac{B+C}{2}\sin\frac{B-C}{2}
=2\sin\alpha\sin d,
\]
and \(\sin2\alpha=2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha\), we obtain
\[
2=\frac{8\cdot 2\sin\alpha\sin d}{2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha}
=\frac{8\sin d}{\cos\alpha}.
\]
So
\[
\sin d=\frac{\cos\alpha}{4}. \tag{4}
\]

Also, from (1),
\[
x=\frac{4\sin\alpha}{\sin(B+\alpha)}.
\]
But
\[
B+\alpha=90^\circ+d,
\]
so
\[
\sin(B+\alpha)=\cos d.
\]
Hence
\[
x=\frac{4\sin\alpha}{\cos d}. \tag{5}
\]

Now use \(AB=x+1\) from (3):
\[
x+1=\frac{8\sin C}{\sin2\alpha}
=\frac{4\sin C}{\sin\alpha\cos\alpha}.
\]
Since
\[
C=90^\circ-\alpha-d,
\]
we have
\[
\sin C=\cos(\alpha+d)=\cos\alpha\cos d-\sin\alpha\sin d.
\]
Thus
\[
x+1=\frac{4(\cos\alpha\cos d-\sin\alpha\sin d)}{\sin\alpha\cos\alpha}
=\frac{4\cos d}{\sin\alpha}-\frac{4\sin d}{\cos\alpha}.
\]
Using (4),
\[
\frac{4\sin d}{\cos\alpha}=1,
\]
so
\[
x+1=\frac{4\cos d}{\sin\alpha}-1,
\]
i.e.
\[
x+2=\frac{4\cos d}{\sin\alpha}. \tag{6}
\]

Multiply (5) and (6):
\[
x(x+2)=16.
\]
So
\[
x^2+2x-16=0.
\]
Hence
\[
x=\frac{-2\pm\sqrt{4+64}}{2}=-1\pm\sqrt{17}.
\]
Since \(x>0\),
\[
x=\sqrt{17}-1.
\]

Therefore,
\[
\boxed{\sqrt{17}-1}.
\]

Let  
\[
\alpha=\frac12\angle BAC.
\]
We will place the triangle in coordinates so that the bisector of \(\angle BAC\) is the \(x\)-axis.

## 1) Coordinates
Set
\[
A=(0,0),\quad B=(m\cos\alpha,m\sin\alpha),\quad C=(n\cos\alpha,-n\sin\alpha),
\]
where
\[
m=AB,\qquad n=AC.
\]

Then since \(AP=1\) and \(AQ=3\),
\[
P=(\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha),\qquad Q=(3\cos\alpha,-3\sin\alpha).
\]

Also the midpoint of \(BC\) is
\[
M=\left(\frac{m+n}{2}\cos\alpha,\frac{m-n}{2}\sin\alpha\right).
\]

We want \(BP=m-1\).

---

## 2) Use \(\angle PMB=\alpha\)

Compute the vectors:
\[
\overrightarrow{MP}=\left(\frac{2-m-n}{2}\cos\alpha,\frac{2-m+n}{2}\sin\alpha\right),
\]
\[
\overrightarrow{MB}=\left(\frac{m-n}{2}\cos\alpha,\frac{m+n}{2}\sin\alpha\right).
\]

Using
\[
\tan\angle(\mathbf u,\mathbf v)=\frac{|\det(\mathbf u,\mathbf v)|}{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v},
\]
and the fact that \(\angle PMB=\alpha\), after simplification we get
\[
2m-m^2+n^2-2n\cos 2\alpha=2n(m-1)(1+\cos2\alpha). \tag{1}
\]

---

## 3) Use \(\angle QMC=\alpha\)

Similarly,
\[
\overrightarrow{MQ}=\left(\frac{6-m-n}{2}\cos\alpha,\frac{n-m-6}{2}\sin\alpha\right),
\]
\[
\overrightarrow{MC}=\left(\frac{n-m}{2}\cos\alpha,-\frac{m+n}{2}\sin\alpha\right).
\]

Using \(\angle QMC=\alpha\), we obtain
\[
m^2-n^2+6n-6m\cos2\alpha=2m(n-3)(1+\cos2\alpha). \tag{2}
\]

---

## 4) Use \(BC=8\)

By the distance formula,
\[
BC^2=m^2+n^2-2mn\cos2\alpha=64. \tag{3}
\]

---

## 5) Eliminate \(\cos2\alpha\)

From (1):
\[
2m-m^2+n^2-2n\cos2\alpha=2n(m-1)+2n(m-1)\cos2\alpha.
\]
Rearrange:
\[
m^2+mn-m-n-32=0,
\]
using (3). Thus
\[
(m-1)(m+n)=32. \tag{4}
\]

From (2), similarly:
\[
n^2+mn-3m-3n-32=0,
\]
so
\[
(n-3)(m+n)=32. \tag{5}
\]

From (4) and (5),
\[
m-1=n-3 \quad\Rightarrow\quad n=m+2.
\]

Plug into (4):
\[
(m-1)(m+n)=(m-1)(2m+2)=32.
\]
So
\[
2(m-1)(m+1)=32
\]
\[
m^2-1=16
\]
\[
m^2=17
\]
\[
m=\sqrt{17}.
\]

Therefore
\[
BP=AB-AP=m-1=\sqrt{17}-1.
\]

\[
\boxed{\sqrt{17}-1}
\]

Let  
\[
\angle BAC=2\theta,\qquad AB=c,\qquad AC=b.
\]
Then \(BP=c-1\), which is what we want.

We use coordinates:

\[
B=(-4,0),\quad C=(4,0),\quad M=(0,0),\quad A=(x,y)\quad (y>0).
\]

Since \(BC=8\), this is valid, and
\[
AB=c,\qquad AC=b.
\]

## 1) Useful formulas for \(x\) and \(\tan\theta\)

From
\[
c^2=(x+4)^2+y^2,\qquad b^2=(x-4)^2+y^2,
\]
subtracting gives
\[
c^2-b^2=16x \quad\Rightarrow\quad x=\frac{c^2-b^2}{16}. \tag{1}
\]

Also, in triangle \(ABC\),
\[
\sin(2\theta)=\frac{2[ABC]}{bc}=\frac{2\cdot \frac12\cdot 8\cdot y}{bc}=\frac{8y}{bc},
\]
and
\[
\cos(2\theta)=\frac{b^2+c^2-64}{2bc}.
\]
Hence
\[
\tan\theta=\frac{\sin(2\theta)}{1+\cos(2\theta)}
=\frac{8y/(bc)}{1+\frac{b^2+c^2-64}{2bc}}
=\frac{16y}{(b+c)^2-64}. \tag{2}
\]

## 2) Coordinates of \(P\) and \(Q\)

Since \(AP=1\) on segment \(AB\),
\[
P=A+\frac1c(B-A)
=\left(x-\frac{x+4}{c},\,y\Bigl(1-\frac1c\Bigr)\right).
\]
So
\[
P=\left(\frac{x(c-1)-4}{c},\,\frac{y(c-1)}{c}\right).
\]

Because \(\angle PMB=\theta\), the line \(MP\) makes angle \(\theta\) with the negative \(x\)-axis, so its slope is \(-\tan\theta\). Thus
\[
\frac{y(c-1)}{x(c-1)-4}=-\tan\theta
\]
or
\[
\tan\theta=\frac{y(c-1)}{4-x(c-1)}. \tag{3}
\]

Similarly, since \(AQ=3\) on segment \(AC\),
\[
Q=A+\frac3b(C-A)
=\left(\frac{x(b-3)+12}{b},\,\frac{y(b-3)}{b}\right).
\]
Because \(\angle QMC=\theta\), line \(MQ\) has slope \(+\tan\theta\), so
\[
\tan\theta=\frac{y(b-3)}{x(b-3)+12}. \tag{4}
\]

## 3) Use (2) with (3)

Equating (2) and (3):
\[
\frac{16}{(b+c)^2-64}=\frac{c-1}{4-x(c-1)}.
\]
Substitute \(x=\frac{c^2-b^2}{16}\) from (1):
\[
64-(c-1)(c^2-b^2)=(c-1)\bigl((b+c)^2-64\bigr).
\]
Simplifying,
\[
64=(c-1)\bigl(2c(b+c)-64\bigr).
\]
So
\[
64c=2c(c-1)(b+c),
\]
hence
\[
b+c=\frac{32}{c-1}. \tag{5}
\]

## 4) Use (2) with (4)

Equating (2) and (4):
\[
\frac{16}{(b+c)^2-64}=\frac{b-3}{x(b-3)+12}.
\]
Again substitute \(x=\frac{c^2-b^2}{16}\), simplify, and get
\[
192=(b-3)\bigl(2b(b+c)-64\bigr),
\]
which becomes
\[
64b=2b(b-3)(b+c),
\]
so
\[
b+c=\frac{32}{b-3}. \tag{6}
\]

From (5) and (6),
\[
\frac{32}{c-1}=\frac{32}{b-3}
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
c-1=b-3
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
b=c+2.
\]

Then
\[
b+c=2c+2.
\]
Plug into (5):
\[
2c+2=\frac{32}{c-1}.
\]
Thus
\[
(c+1)(c-1)=16
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
c^2=17
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
c=\sqrt{17}.
\]

Therefore
\[
BP=AB-AP=c-1=\sqrt{17}-1.
\]

\[
\boxed{\sqrt{17}-1}
\]

Let  
\[
\angle BAC=2\alpha,\qquad x=BP.
\]

We will show \(x\) is determined uniquely.

### 1) First find the side lengths
Since \(AP=1\), we have
\[
AB=x+1.
\]

Now let \(CQ=y\). In \(\triangle BMP\), by the Law of Sines,
\[
\frac{BP}{\sin\alpha}=\frac{BM}{\sin\angle BPM}
\]
and since \(BM=4\) and
\[
\angle BPM=180^\circ-\angle ABC-\alpha=C+\alpha,
\]
we get
\[
x=\frac{4\sin\alpha}{\sin(C+\alpha)}.
\]

Similarly in \(\triangle CQM\),
\[
y=\frac{4\sin\alpha}{\sin(B+\alpha)}.
\]

But
\[
(B+\alpha)+(C+\alpha)=B+C+2\alpha=180^\circ,
\]
so
\[
\sin(B+\alpha)=\sin(C+\alpha).
\]
Hence
\[
y=x.
\]

Therefore
\[
CQ=BP=x,\qquad AC=AQ+CQ=3+x.
\]

So the three sides of \(\triangle ABC\) are
\[
AB=x+1,\quad AC=x+3,\quad BC=8.
\]

---

### 2) Put the triangle in coordinates
Set
\[
B=(-4,0),\quad C=(4,0),\quad M=(0,0).
\]
Let \(A=(u,h)\).

Using
\[
AB=x+1,\qquad AC=x+3,
\]
the \(x\)-coordinate of \(A\) is
\[
u=\frac{AB^2-AC^2}{16}
=\frac{(x+1)^2-(x+3)^2}{16}
=-\frac{x+2}{4}.
\]

From Heron's formula, with semiperimeter
\[
s=\frac{8+(x+1)+(x+3)}2=x+6,
\]
the area is
\[
[K]^2=s(s-8)(s-(x+1))(s-(x+3))
=(x+6)(x-2)\cdot 5\cdot 3.
\]
Since the area is also \(\frac12\cdot 8\cdot h=4h\),
\[
16h^2=15(x+6)(x-2),
\]
so
\[
h=\frac{\sqrt{15(x+6)(x-2)}}4.
\]

Thus
\[
A=\left(-\frac{x+2}{4},\ \frac{\sqrt{15(x+6)(x-2)}}4\right).
\]

---

### 3) Coordinates of \(P\)
Point \(P\) is 1 unit from \(A\) toward \(B\) along \(AB\), and \(AB=x+1\). Hence
\[
P=A+\frac1{x+1}(B-A).
\]
This gives
\[
P_x=-\frac{x^2+2x+16}{4(x+1)},\qquad
P_y=\frac{x}{x+1}\,h.
\]

Since \(\angle PMB=\alpha\), the line \(MP\) makes angle \(\alpha\) with the negative \(x\)-axis, so
\[
\tan\alpha=\frac{P_y}{-P_x}
=\frac{4xh}{x^2+2x+16}.
\]

Substitute \(h\):
\[
\tan\alpha=
\frac{x\sqrt{15(x+6)(x-2)}}{x^2+2x+16}.
\]

---

### 4) Also compute \(\tan\alpha\) from the sides
In \(\triangle ABC\), since \(\angle A=2\alpha\),
\[
\tan\alpha=\tan\frac A2
=\sqrt{\frac{(s-b)(s-c)}{s(s-a)}}
\]
with
\[
a=8,\quad b=x+3,\quad c=x+1,\quad s=x+6.
\]
So
\[
\tan\alpha
=\sqrt{\frac{3\cdot 5}{(x+6)(x-2)}}
=\sqrt{\frac{15}{(x+6)(x-2)}}.
\]

Equate the two expressions for \(\tan\alpha\):
\[
\frac{x\sqrt{15(x+6)(x-2)}}{x^2+2x+16}
=
\sqrt{\frac{15}{(x+6)(x-2)}}.
\]

Cancel \(\sqrt{15}\):
\[
x(x+6)(x-2)=x^2+2x+16.
\]

Expand:
\[
x^3+3x^2-14x-16=0.
\]

Factor:
\[
(x+1)(x^2+2x-16)=0.
\]

The positive solution is
\[
x=-1+\sqrt{17}.
\]

Therefore
\[
BP=x=\sqrt{17}-1.
\]

\[
\boxed{\sqrt{17}-1}
\]

Let  
\[
\angle BAC=2\alpha,\qquad x=BP.
\]
Since \(M\) is the midpoint of \(BC\), we have
\[
MB=MC=\frac{BC}{2}=4.
\]

Also,
\[
AB=AP+PB=1+x.
\]

We will show \(QC=x\), so then
\[
AC=AQ+QC=3+x.
\]

## 1) Use the condition at \(M\)

In \(\triangle MBP\),

- \(\angle PMB=\alpha\),
- \(\angle MBP=\angle ABC=B\),
- hence \(\angle MPB=180^\circ-(\alpha+B)\).

By the Law of Sines,
\[
\frac{x}{\sin\alpha}=\frac{4}{\sin(\alpha+B)},
\]
so
\[
x=\frac{4\sin\alpha}{\sin(\alpha+B)}.
\]

Similarly, in \(\triangle MQC\),

- \(\angle QMC=\alpha\),
- \(\angle MCQ=\angle ACB=C\),

so
\[
QC=\frac{4\sin\alpha}{\sin(\alpha+C)}.
\]

Now since \(B+C=180^\circ-2\alpha\),
\[
\alpha+B=90^\circ+\frac{B-C}{2},\qquad
\alpha+C=90^\circ+\frac{C-B}{2}.
\]
Thus
\[
\sin(\alpha+B)=\sin(\alpha+C)=\cos\frac{B-C}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
QC=x.
\]

So indeed
\[
AB=x+1,\qquad AC=x+3.
\]

---

## 2) Express \(AB+AC\) using the big triangle

By the Law of Sines in \(\triangle ABC\),
\[
\frac{AB}{\sin C}=\frac{AC}{\sin B}=\frac{BC}{\sin 2\alpha}=\frac{8}{\sin 2\alpha}.
\]
Hence
\[
AB+AC=\frac{8(\sin B+\sin C)}{\sin 2\alpha}.
\]
Using
\[
\sin B+\sin C=2\sin\frac{B+C}{2}\cos\frac{B-C}{2}
=2\cos\alpha\cos\frac{B-C}{2},
\]
we get
\[
AB+AC
=\frac{8\cdot 2\cos\alpha\cos\frac{B-C}{2}}{2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha}
=\frac{8\cos\frac{B-C}{2}}{\sin\alpha}.
\]

But from step 1,
\[
x=\frac{4\sin\alpha}{\cos\frac{B-C}{2}}.
\]
Multiplying these two formulas,
\[
x(AB+AC)=32.
\]

Since
\[
AB+AC=(x+1)+(x+3)=2x+4,
\]
we have
\[
x(2x+4)=32.
\]
So
\[
x(x+2)=16
\]
and
\[
x^2+2x-16=0.
\]
Thus
\[
x=\frac{-2\pm\sqrt{4+64}}{2}=-1\pm\sqrt{17}.
\]
Since \(x>0\),
\[
x=\sqrt{17}-1.
\]

Therefore,
\[
\boxed{\sqrt{17}-1}.
\]

Let  
\[
\angle BAC = 2\alpha.
\]
Then the given conditions are
\[
\angle PMB=\angle QMC=\alpha.
\]

We want \(BP\).

---

### 1. Use the sine rule in \(\triangle MBP\)

Since \(M\) is the midpoint of \(BC\) and \(BC=8\),
\[
MB=MC=4.
\]

In \(\triangle MBP\),

- \(\angle PMB=\alpha\),
- \(\angle MBP=\angle ABC=:B\) because \(P\) lies on \(AB\).

So by the Law of Sines,
\[
\frac{BP}{\sin\alpha}=\frac{MB}{\sin(\alpha+B)}=\frac{4}{\sin(\alpha+B)}.
\]
Hence
\[
BP=\frac{4\sin\alpha}{\sin(\alpha+B)}.
\]
Let
\[
t=BP.
\]
Then
\[
t=\frac{4\sin\alpha}{\sin(\alpha+B)}. \tag{1}
\]

---

### 2. Use the sine rule in \(\triangle MCQ\)

Similarly, in \(\triangle MCQ\),

- \(\angle QMC=\alpha\),
- \(\angle MCQ=\angle ACB=:C\),
- \(CQ=AC-AQ=AC-3\).

Thus
\[
\frac{AC-3}{\sin\alpha}=\frac{4}{\sin(\alpha+C)},
\]
so
\[
AC-3=\frac{4\sin\alpha}{\sin(\alpha+C)}. \tag{2}
\]

Now note that
\[
B+C=180^\circ-2\alpha.
\]
So if \(\delta=\frac{B-C}{2}\), then
\[
B=90^\circ-\alpha+\delta,\qquad C=90^\circ-\alpha-\delta.
\]
Therefore
\[
\alpha+B=90^\circ+\delta,\qquad \alpha+C=90^\circ-\delta,
\]
and hence
\[
\sin(\alpha+B)=\sin(\alpha+C)=\cos\delta.
\]
Comparing (1) and (2), we get
\[
BP=AC-3.
\]
Thus
\[
AC=t+3.
\]
Also since \(AP=1\),
\[
AB=AP+BP=1+t.
\]

So
\[
AB=t+1,\qquad AC=t+3. \tag{3}
\]

---

### 3. Express \(AB+AC\) using the Law of Sines in \(\triangle ABC\)

By the Law of Sines in \(\triangle ABC\),
\[
\frac{AB}{\sin C}=\frac{AC}{\sin B}=\frac{BC}{\sin 2\alpha}=\frac{8}{\sin 2\alpha}.
\]
Hence
\[
AB+AC=\frac{8(\sin B+\sin C)}{\sin 2\alpha}.
\]
Using
\[
\sin B+\sin C
=2\sin\frac{B+C}{2}\cos\frac{B-C}{2}
=2\sin(90^\circ-\alpha)\cos\delta
=2\cos\alpha\cos\delta,
\]
we obtain
\[
AB+AC=\frac{8\cdot 2\cos\alpha\cos\delta}{2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha}
=\frac{8\cos\delta}{\sin\alpha}. \tag{4}
\]

From (1),
\[
t=\frac{4\sin\alpha}{\cos\delta}.
\]
Multiply this with (4):
\[
t(AB+AC)=\frac{4\sin\alpha}{\cos\delta}\cdot \frac{8\cos\delta}{\sin\alpha}=32.
\]
Using (3),
\[
AB+AC=(t+1)+(t+3)=2t+4.
\]
So
\[
t(2t+4)=32.
\]
Thus
\[
2t^2+4t-32=0
\]
\[
t^2+2t-16=0.
\]
Solving:
\[
t=\frac{-2\pm\sqrt{4+64}}{2}=-1\pm\sqrt{17}.
\]
Since \(t=BP>0\),
\[
t=\sqrt{17}-1.
\]

Therefore,
\[
\boxed{\sqrt{17}-1}.
\]

Let  
\[
AB=AC=1,\qquad AP=x,\qquad AQ=y.
\]

Since
\[
\angle BAP=20^\circ,\quad \angle PAQ=46^\circ,\quad \angle QAC=26^\circ,
\]
we also have
\[
\angle BAC=92^\circ,\qquad \angle PAC=72^\circ.
\]

## 1) Use the equal-length condition
Given
\[
BP=PQ=QC,
\]
apply the Law of Cosines in \(\triangle ABP\), \(\triangle APQ\), and \(\triangle AQC\):

\[
BP^2=1+x^2-2x\cos20^\circ,
\]
\[
PQ^2=x^2+y^2-2xy\cos46^\circ,
\]
\[
QC^2=1+y^2-2y\cos26^\circ.
\]

Since these are equal, we get the system
\[
1+x^2-2x\cos20^\circ=x^2+y^2-2xy\cos46^\circ,
\]
\[
x^2+y^2-2xy\cos46^\circ=1+y^2-2y\cos26^\circ.
\]

Solving for the positive interior solution gives
\[
x=\frac{\sin34^\circ}{\sin74^\circ},\qquad
y=\frac{\sin40^\circ}{\sin74^\circ}.
\]

So
\[
AP=\frac{\sin34^\circ}{\sin74^\circ}.
\]

## 2) Look at triangle \(APC\)
In \(\triangle APC\), we know
\[
AC=1,\qquad AP=\frac{\sin34^\circ}{\sin74^\circ},\qquad \angle PAC=72^\circ.
\]

Now compute \(PC\) with the Law of Cosines:
\[
PC^2=AP^2+AC^2-2(AP)(AC)\cos72^\circ.
\]
Substituting \(AP=\dfrac{\sin34^\circ}{\sin74^\circ}\) simplifies to
\[
PC=\frac{\sin72^\circ}{\sin74^\circ}.
\]

Thus the three sides of \(\triangle APC\) are in the ratio
\[
AP:PC:AC=\sin34^\circ:\sin72^\circ:\sin74^\circ.
\]

By the Law of Sines, the angles opposite these sides are therefore
\[
34^\circ,\ 72^\circ,\ 74^\circ.
\]

Since side \(AC\) is opposite \(\angle APC\), we conclude
\[
\angle APC=74^\circ.
\]

\[
\boxed{74}
\]

Let  
\[
AB=AC=1
\]
by scaling the triangle. Set
\[
AP=p,\qquad AQ=q,\qquad BP=PQ=QC=r.
\]

Also,
\[
\angle BAC=20^\circ+46^\circ+26^\circ=92^\circ,
\]
so in triangle \(APC\),
\[
\angle PAC=46^\circ+26^\circ=72^\circ.
\]

## 1) Use the Law of Cosines on the three equal segments
From triangles \(ABP,\ APQ,\ AQC\):

\[
r^2=1+p^2-2p\cos20^\circ \tag{1}
\]
\[
r^2=p^2+q^2-2pq\cos46^\circ \tag{2}
\]
\[
r^2=1+q^2-2q\cos26^\circ \tag{3}
\]

Subtracting (2) from (1) and (3) from (2) gives
\[
1-q^2-2p\cos20^\circ+2pq\cos46^\circ=0 \tag{4}
\]
\[
1-p^2-2q\cos26^\circ+2pq\cos46^\circ=0 \tag{5}
\]

## 2) Solve for \(p,q\)
We claim
\[
p=\frac{\sin34^\circ}{\sin74^\circ},\qquad q=\frac{\sin40^\circ}{\sin74^\circ}.
\]

Check (4): multiply by \(\sin^2 74^\circ\):
\[
\sin^2 74-\sin^2 40-2\sin34\sin74\cos20+2\sin34\sin40\cos46.
\]
Using identities
\[
\sin^2A-\sin^2B=\sin(A+B)\sin(A-B),\quad
2\sin x\cos y=\sin(x+y)+\sin(x-y),
\]
this becomes
\[
\sin34(\sin66-\sin54-\sin6)=0,
\]
since \(\sin94=\sin86\). And indeed
\[
\sin66-\sin6=2\cos36\sin30=\cos36=\sin54.
\]
So (4) holds.

Similarly for (5), after multiplying by \(\sin^2 74^\circ\), it becomes
\[
\sin40(\sin72-\sin48-\sin12)=0,
\]
since \(\sin100=\sin80\), and
\[
\sin72-\sin12=2\cos42\sin30=\cos42=\sin48.
\]
So (5) also holds.

Thus
\[
\frac{AP}{AC}=p=\frac{\sin34^\circ}{\sin74^\circ}. \tag{6}
\]

## 3) Use triangle \(APC\)
Let
\[
\angle APC=\theta.
\]
Then in triangle \(APC\),
\[
\angle PAC=72^\circ,\qquad \angle ACP=108^\circ-\theta.
\]
By the Law of Sines,
\[
\frac{AP}{AC}=\frac{\sin(108^\circ-\theta)}{\sin\theta}. \tag{7}
\]

From (6) and (7),
\[
\frac{\sin(108^\circ-\theta)}{\sin\theta}=\frac{\sin34^\circ}{\sin74^\circ}.
\]

Now if \(\theta=74^\circ\), then
\[
108^\circ-\theta=34^\circ,
\]
and the ratio matches exactly:
\[
\frac{\sin34^\circ}{\sin74^\circ}.
\]
This is the valid solution.

Therefore,
\[
\angle APC=74^\circ.
\]

\boxed{74}
\]

Let \(AB=AC=s\).  
Set
\[
AP=xs,\qquad AQ=ys.
\]
We will determine \(x\), then use triangle \(APC\).

## 1) Use the equal-length condition

Since
\[
BP=PQ=QC,
\]
apply the Law of Cosines in triangles \(ABP,\ APQ,\ AQC\):

\[
BP^2=s^2+x^2s^2-2sx s\cos20^\circ,
\]
\[
PQ^2=x^2s^2+y^2s^2-2xys^2\cos46^\circ,
\]
\[
QC^2=s^2+y^2s^2-2sy s\cos26^\circ.
\]

Divide by \(s^2\). Then
\[
1+x^2-2x\cos20^\circ=x^2+y^2-2xy\cos46^\circ=1+y^2-2y\cos26^\circ.
\]

So
\[
x^2-2xy\cos46^\circ+2y\cos26^\circ=1 \tag{1}
\]
and
\[
y^2-2xy\cos46^\circ+2x\cos20^\circ=1. \tag{2}
\]

## 2) A trig solution to the system

Claim:
\[
x=\frac{\sin34^\circ}{\sin74^\circ},\qquad
y=\frac{\sin40^\circ}{\sin74^\circ}.
\]

We verify.

### Check (1)

Substitute into (1) and multiply by \(\sin^2 74^\circ\). We need to show
\[
\sin^2 34^\circ-2\cos46^\circ\sin34^\circ\sin40^\circ
+2\cos26^\circ\sin40^\circ\sin74^\circ-\sin^2 74^\circ=0.
\]

Rewrite as
\[
(\sin^2 34^\circ-\sin^2 74^\circ)
+2\sin40^\circ(\cos26^\circ\sin74^\circ-\cos46^\circ\sin34^\circ).
\]

Now
\[
\sin^2 74^\circ-\sin^2 34^\circ
=\sin(108^\circ)\sin40^\circ
=\sin72^\circ\sin40^\circ
=\sin40^\circ\cos18^\circ,
\]
and
\[
2(\cos26^\circ\sin74^\circ-\cos46^\circ\sin34^\circ)
=(\sin100^\circ+\sin48^\circ)-(\sin80^\circ-\sin12^\circ)
=\sin48^\circ+\sin12^\circ
=\cos18^\circ.
\]
So the whole expression is
\[
-\sin40^\circ\cos18^\circ+\sin40^\circ\cos18^\circ=0.
\]

### Check (2)

Similarly, substituting into (2) and multiplying by \(\sin^2 74^\circ\), we need
\[
\sin^2 40^\circ-2\cos46^\circ\sin34^\circ\sin40^\circ
+2\cos20^\circ\sin34^\circ\sin74^\circ-\sin^2 74^\circ=0.
\]
This becomes
\[
(\sin^2 40^\circ-\sin^2 74^\circ)
+2\sin34^\circ(\cos20^\circ\sin74^\circ-\cos46^\circ\sin40^\circ).
\]

Now
\[
\sin^2 74^\circ-\sin^2 40^\circ
=\sin114^\circ\sin34^\circ
=\sin66^\circ\sin34^\circ
=\cos24^\circ\sin34^\circ,
\]
and
\[
2(\cos20^\circ\sin74^\circ-\cos46^\circ\sin40^\circ)
=(\sin94^\circ+\sin54^\circ)-(\sin86^\circ-\sin6^\circ)
=\sin54^\circ+\sin6^\circ
=\cos24^\circ.
\]
So again the expression is \(0\).

Thus
\[
\frac{AP}{AC}=x=\frac{\sin34^\circ}{\sin74^\circ}. \tag{3}
\]

## 3) Now use triangle \(APC\)

At \(A\),
\[
\angle PAC=\angle PAQ+\angle QAC=46^\circ+26^\circ=72^\circ.
\]

Let
\[
\angle APC=\theta.
\]
Then in triangle \(APC\),
\[
\angle ACP=180^\circ-72^\circ-\theta=108^\circ-\theta.
\]

By the Law of Sines in triangle \(APC\),
\[
\frac{AP}{AC}=\frac{\sin\angle ACP}{\sin\angle APC}
=\frac{\sin(108^\circ-\theta)}{\sin\theta}.
\]
Using (3),
\[
\frac{\sin(108^\circ-\theta)}{\sin\theta}
=\frac{\sin34^\circ}{\sin74^\circ}.
\]

The angle pair
\[
\angle ACP=34^\circ,\qquad \angle APC=74^\circ
\]
satisfies both this ratio and the sum \(34^\circ+74^\circ=108^\circ\).

Therefore,
\[
\angle APC=74^\circ.
\]

\boxed{74}
