f(2015)f(2016)=2015\cdot 1008\cdot ab.
\]

Factor:
\[
2015=5\cdot 13\cdot 31,\qquad 1008=2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 7.
\]
Hence
\[
2015\cdot 1008 = 2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31.
\]
Its squarefree part is
\[
5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31=14105.
\]

Therefore \(ab\) must be \(14105\) times a square. To minimize \(a+b\), we take the smallest possible product:
\[
ab=14105.
\]

Now factor
\[
14105=5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31.
\]
We need the factor pair with smallest sum. The closest pair is
\[
91=7\cdot 13,\qquad 155=5\cdot 31,
\]
so
\[
a+b=91+155=246.
\]

Thus the minimum possible value of
\[
f(1)+f(2)=a+b
\]
is

\[
\boxed{246}.
\]

Let

\[
P(x,y):\quad f(x^2-3y^2)+f(x^2+y^2)=2(x+y)f(x-y)
\]

for all \(x,y\in\mathbb Z\).

We want the minimum of \(f(1)+f(2)\).

---

## 1. First basic values

From \(P(0,0)\),

\[
2f(0)=0 \implies f(0)=0.
\]

From \(P(x,0)\),

\[
f(x^2)+f(x^2)=2x f(x)
\]

so

\[
f(x^2)=x f(x)\qquad (\forall x\in\mathbb Z). \tag{1}
\]

---

## 2. Prove that \(f\) is odd

Use \(P(0,y)\):

\[
f(-3y^2)+f(y^2)=2y f(-y).
\]

Use \(P(0,-y)\):

\[
f(-3y^2)+f(y^2)=-2y f(y).
\]

Subtracting gives, for \(y\neq 0\),

\[
2y f(-y)=-2y f(y)\implies f(-y)=-f(y).
\]

Together with \(f(0)=0\), this shows

\[
f(-n)=-f(n)\qquad (\forall n\in\mathbb Z). \tag{2}
\]

---

## 3. A key recurrence from \(y=1\) and \(y=-1\)

From \(P(x,1)\),

\[
f(x^2-3)+f(x^2+1)=2(x+1)f(x-1).
\]

From \(P(x,-1)\),

\[
f(x^2-3)+f(x^2+1)=2(x-1)f(x+1).
\]

Hence

\[
(x+1)f(x-1)=(x-1)f(x+1)\qquad (\forall x\in\mathbb Z). \tag{3}
\]

This relates values of the same parity.

---

## 4. Determine \(f\) on odd and even integers

Let \(a=f(1)\) and \(b=f(2)\).

### Odd integers
Set \(x=2\) in (3):

\[
3f(1)=f(3)\implies f(3)=3a.
\]

Then set \(x=4\):

\[
5f(3)=3f(5)\implies f(5)=5a.
\]

By induction using even \(x=2k\), we get

\[
f(2k+1)=(2k+1)a\qquad (k\ge 0).
\]

Using oddness (2), this holds for all odd integers \(n\):

\[
f(n)=an \qquad (n\text{ odd}). \tag{4}
\]

### Even integers
Set \(x=3\) in (3):

\[
4f(2)=2f(4)\implies f(4)=2b.
\]

Then \(x=5\):

\[
6f(4)=4f(6)\implies f(6)=3b.
\]

Inductively,

\[
f(2k)=kb \qquad (k\ge 1).
\]

Using oddness again, for all even integers \(n\),

\[
f(n)=\frac n2\, b \qquad (n\text{ even}). \tag{5}
\]

So every solution must have the form

\[
f(n)=
\begin{cases}
an,& n\text{ odd},\\[4pt]
\dfrac n2\,b,& n\text{ even},
\end{cases}
\qquad a,b\in\mathbb Z.
\]

Because \(f(n)>0\) for \(n>0\), we must have

\[
a>0,\qquad b>0. \tag{6}
\]

---

## 5. Check that this form really satisfies the FE

Note that

\[
x^2-3y^2 \equiv x-y \pmod 2,\qquad x^2+y^2 \equiv x+y \equiv x-y \pmod 2.
\]

So the three arguments \(x^2-3y^2,\ x^2+y^2,\ x-y\) always have the same parity.

- If they are odd, then \(f(n)=an\) on all three, and
  \[
  f(x^2-3y^2)+f(x^2+y^2)
  =a[(x^2-3y^2)+(x^2+y^2)]
  =2a(x^2-y^2)
  =2(x+y)a(x-y).
  \]

- If they are even, then \(f(n)=\frac n2 b\) on all three, and
  \[
  f(x^2-3y^2)+f(x^2+y^2)
  =\frac b2[(x^2-3y^2)+(x^2+y^2)]
  =b(x^2-y^2)
  =2(x+y)\frac{x-y}{2}b.
  \]

So every such pair \((a,b)\) indeed gives a valid solution.

Thus all solutions are exactly those above.

---

## 6. Use the perfect-square condition

Since \(2015\) is odd and \(2016\) is even,

\[
f(2015)=2015a,\qquad f(2016)=1008b.
\]

Thus

\[
f(2015)f(2016)=2015\cdot 1008\cdot ab.
\]

Factor:

\[
2015=5\cdot 13\cdot 31,\qquad 1008=2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 7.
\]

Hence

\[
2015\cdot 1008=2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31.
\]

For this times \(ab\) to be a perfect square, \(ab\) must be

\[
ab=(5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31)\,t^2=14105\,t^2
\]

for some positive integer \(t\).

To minimize \(a+b\), clearly \(t=1\); otherwise \(ab\ge 14105\cdot 4\), which already makes the sum much larger.

So we need positive integers \(a,b\) with

\[
ab=14105=5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31
\]

and minimum \(a+b\).

Factor pairs of \(14105\):

\[
1\cdot 14105,\quad 5\cdot 2821,\quad 7\cdot 2015,\quad 13\cdot 1085,
\]
\[
31\cdot 455,\quad 35\cdot 403,\quad 65\cdot 217,\quad 91\cdot 155.
\]

The smallest sum is

\[
91+155=246.
\]

This is achievable, e.g. with \(a=91,\ b=155\).

Then

\[
f(1)+f(2)=a+b=246.
\]

\[
\boxed{246}
\]

Let

\[
P(x,y):\quad f(x^2-3y^2)+f(x^2+y^2)=2(x+y)f(x-y)
\]

for all integers \(x,y\).

We want the minimum of \(f(1)+f(2)\).

---

## 1. First basic identities

### From \(P(0,0)\)
\[
f(0)+f(0)=0 \implies f(0)=0.
\]

### From \(P(x,0)\)
\[
f(x^2)+f(x^2)=2x f(x)
\]
so
\[
f(x^2)=x f(x)\qquad (\forall x\in \mathbb Z).
\]

Now compare \(x\) and \(-x\):

\[
f(x^2)=x f(x)=(-x)f(-x).
\]

Hence for \(x\neq 0\),
\[
f(-x)=-f(x).
\]
Together with \(f(0)=0\), this shows:

\[
\boxed{f \text{ is odd}.}
\]

---

## 2. A recurrence from \(y=1\) and \(y=-1\)

Using \(P(x,1)\):

\[
f(x^2-3)+f(x^2+1)=2(x+1)f(x-1).
\]

Using \(P(x,-1)\):

\[
f(x^2-3)+f(x^2+1)=2(x-1)f(x+1).
\]

So for all integers \(x\),

\[
(x+1)f(x-1)=(x-1)f(x+1).
\]

Thus
\[
\boxed{(x+1)f(x-1)=(x-1)f(x+1)}.
\]

---

## 3. Determine \(f\) on odd and even integers

Let
\[
a=f(1),\qquad b=f(2).
\]
Since \(n>0\Rightarrow f(n)>0\), we have
\[
a>0,\qquad b>0.
\]

### Odd integers
Take \(x=2k\). Then

\[
(2k+1)f(2k-1)=(2k-1)f(2k+1).
\]

Starting from \(f(1)=a\), this gives by induction

\[
f(2k+1)=(2k+1)a \qquad (k\ge 0).
\]

Because \(f\) is odd, this extends to all odd integers \(n\):

\[
\boxed{f(n)=an \quad \text{for odd } n.}
\]

### Even integers
Take \(x=2k+1\). Then

\[
(2k+2)f(2k)=2k\,f(2k+2).
\]

Starting from \(f(2)=b\), this gives by induction

\[
f(2k)=kb \qquad (k\ge 1).
\]

So for all even integers \(n\),

\[
\boxed{f(n)=\frac b2\,n.}
\]

Thus the general form is:

\[
f(n)=
\begin{cases}
an, & n \text{ odd},\\[4pt]
\dfrac b2\,n, & n \text{ even},
\end{cases}
\qquad a,b\in \mathbb Z_{>0}.
\]

---

## 4. Check this form really works

Note that modulo \(2\),

\[
x^2-3y^2 \equiv x-y,\qquad x^2+y^2\equiv x-y \pmod 2.
\]

So the three integers
\[
x^2-3y^2,\quad x^2+y^2,\quad x-y
\]
always have the same parity.

Therefore the same coefficient is used on both sides:

- if they are odd, coefficient \(a\),
- if they are even, coefficient \(\frac b2\).

Then the equation becomes

\[
c(x^2-3y^2)+c(x^2+y^2)=2(x+y)\,c(x-y),
\]
i.e.
\[
c(2x^2-2y^2)=2c(x+y)(x-y),
\]
which is true.

So every such pair \((a,b)\) indeed gives a valid function.

---

## 5. Use the square condition

Since \(2015\) is odd and \(2016\) is even,

\[
f(2015)=2015a,
\qquad
f(2016)=\frac b2\cdot 2016=1008b.
\]

Hence

\[
f(2015)f(2016)=2015\cdot 1008\cdot ab.
\]

Factor:

\[
2015=5\cdot 13\cdot 31,\qquad
1008=2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 7.
\]

So

\[
2015\cdot 1008 = 2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31.
\]

The square part \(2^4\cdot 3^2\) is harmless, so we need

\[
5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31 \cdot ab
\]
to be a perfect square.

Let
\[
K=5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31=14105.
\]

Since \(K\) is squarefree, for \(Kab\) to be a square, each prime in \(K\) must divide \(ab\). Thus

\[
K\mid ab.
\]

So the smallest possible \(ab\) is \(14105\).

To minimize \(a+b\), we should first take the smallest possible product, namely \(ab=14105\), and then choose factors as close as possible.

Factor pairs of \(14105\):
\[
1\cdot 14105,\;
5\cdot 2821,\;
7\cdot 2015,\;
13\cdot 1085,\;
31\cdot 455,\;
35\cdot 403,\;
65\cdot 217,\;
91\cdot 155.
\]

The closest pair is
\[
91,\;155,
\]
giving
\[
a+b=91+155=246.
\]

---

## 6. Attainability

Take
\[
a=91,\qquad b=155.
\]

Then define
\[
f(n)=
\begin{cases}
91n, & n\text{ odd},\\[4pt]
\dfrac{155}{2}n, & n\text{ even}.
\end{cases}
\]

This is integer-valued, positive for \(n>0\), satisfies the functional equation, and

\[
f(2015)f(2016)
\]
is a perfect square.

Therefore the minimum is

\[
\boxed{246}.
\]

\[
\boxed{246}
\]

Let
\[
A=f(1),\qquad B=f(2).
\]
We will determine the form of \(f\), then use the square condition.

## 1. First consequences of the functional equation

We are given
\[
f(x^2-3y^2)+f(x^2+y^2)=2(x+y)f(x-y)\qquad(\forall x,y\in\mathbb Z).
\]

### Set \(y=0\)
Then
\[
f(x^2)+f(x^2)=2x f(x),
\]
so
\[
f(x^2)=x f(x)\qquad(\forall x\in\mathbb Z).
\]

In particular, with \(x=0\),
\[
f(0)=0.
\]

Now for \(t>0\),
\[
f(t^2)=t f(t),
\]
but also
\[
f(t^2)=(-t)f(-t).
\]
Hence
\[
t f(t)=-t f(-t)\implies f(-t)=-f(t).
\]
So \(f\) is odd:
\[
f(-n)=-f(n)\qquad(\forall n\in\mathbb Z).
\]

---

## 2. A recurrence

Now set \(y=1\). Then
\[
f(x^2-3)+f(x^2+1)=2(x+1)f(x-1). \tag{1}
\]

Set \(y=-1\). Then
\[
f(x^2-3)+f(x^2+1)=2(x-1)f(x+1). \tag{2}
\]

Comparing (1) and (2),
\[
2(x+1)f(x-1)=2(x-1)f(x+1),
\]
so
\[
(x+1)f(x-1)=(x-1)f(x+1).
\]

Let \(n=x-1\). Then
\[
(n+2)f(n)=n f(n+2)\qquad(\forall n\in\mathbb Z). \tag{3}
\]

---

## 3. Determine \(f\) on odd and even integers

From (3):

- For odd positive integers:
  \[
  3f(1)=f(3),\quad 5f(3)=3f(5),\quad \dots
  \]
  which gives
  \[
  f(2k+1)=(2k+1)A.
  \]

- For even positive integers:
  \[
  4f(2)=2f(4),\quad 6f(4)=4f(6),\quad \dots
  \]
  which gives
  \[
  f(2k)=kB.
  \]

Using oddness, this extends to all integers:
\[
f(n)=
\begin{cases}
An, & n \text{ odd},\\[4pt]
\dfrac{B}{2}n, & n \text{ even}.
\end{cases}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
f(2k+1)=(2k+1)A,\qquad f(2k)=kB.
\]

Since \(f(n)>0\) for all \(n>0\), we must have
\[
A>0,\qquad B>0.
\]

---

## 4. Square condition

We have
\[
f(2015)=2015A
\]
since \(2015\) is odd, and
\[
f(2016)=1008B
\]
since \(2016=2\cdot 1008\).

Thus
\[
f(2015)f(2016)=2015\cdot 1008\cdot A\cdot B.
\]

Factor:
\[
2015=5\cdot 13\cdot 31,\qquad 1008=2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 7.
\]
So
\[
2015\cdot 1008=2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31.
\]

For this to be a perfect square, the odd-prime part
\[
5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31=14105
\]
must be supplied by \(AB\) modulo squares.

To minimize \(A+B\), we should not include any unnecessary square factors in \(A\) or \(B\). So at minimum, \(A\) and \(B\) are complementary divisors of
\[
14105=5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31.
\]

Thus we minimize \(A+B\) over factor pairs of \(14105\):
\[
1+14105,\;
5+2821,\;
7+2015,\;
13+1085,\;
31+455,\;
35+403,\;
65+217,\;
91+155.
\]
The smallest sum is
\[
91+155=246.
\]

This is achievable, e.g. \((A,B)=(91,155)\).

Therefore,
\[
\min\bigl(f(1)+f(2)\bigr)=A+B=246.
\]

\[
\boxed{246}
\]

Let  
\[
P(x,y):\quad f(x^2-3y^2)+f(x^2+y^2)=2(x+y)f(x-y)
\]
for all integers \(x,y\).

We want the minimum possible value of \(f(1)+f(2)\).

---

## 1. First basic identities

### From \(y=0\)
Using \(P(x,0)\):
\[
f(x^2)+f(x^2)=2x f(x),
\]
so
\[
\boxed{f(x^2)=x f(x)} \qquad (\forall x\in\mathbb Z).
\]
In particular, with \(x=0\),
\[
f(0)=0.
\]

---

## 2. Show that \(f\) is odd

Use \(P(0,y)\):
\[
f(-3y^2)+f(y^2)=2y f(-y).
\]
Use \(P(0,-y)\):
\[
f(-3y^2)+f(y^2)=-2y f(y).
\]
Comparing gives, for \(y\neq 0\),
\[
f(-y)=-f(y).
\]
Together with \(f(0)=0\), this yields
\[
\boxed{f(-n)=-f(n)} \qquad (\forall n\in\mathbb Z).
\]

---

## 3. A recurrence from \(y=1\) and \(y=-1\)

From \(P(x,1)\):
\[
f(x^2-3)+f(x^2+1)=2(x+1)f(x-1).
\]
From \(P(x,-1)\):
\[
f(x^2-3)+f(x^2+1)=2(x-1)f(x+1).
\]
Hence
\[
2(x+1)f(x-1)=2(x-1)f(x+1),
\]
so
\[
\boxed{(x+1)f(x-1)=(x-1)f(x+1)} \qquad (\forall x\in\mathbb Z).
\]

This connects values of the same parity.

---

## 4. Determine all possible forms of \(f\)

Let
\[
a=f(1),\qquad b=f(2).
\]
Since \(f(n)>0\) for \(n>0\), we know \(a,b>0\).

### Odd integers
Take \(x=2k\) in the recurrence:
\[
(2k+1)f(2k-1)=(2k-1)f(2k+1).
\]
Starting from \(f(1)=a\), induction gives
\[
f(3)=3a,\ f(5)=5a,\ \dots
\]
so for every positive odd \(n\),
\[
f(n)=an.
\]

### Even integers
Take \(x=2k+1\):
\[
(2k+2)f(2k)=2k\,f(2k+2),
\]
or
\[
(k+1)f(2k)=k f(2k+2).
\]
Starting from \(f(2)=b\), induction gives
\[
f(4)=2b,\ f(6)=3b,\ \dots
\]
so for every positive even \(n=2m\),
\[
f(n)=mb=\frac b2\,n.
\]

Using oddness for negative integers, the full form is
\[
\boxed{
f(n)=
\begin{cases}
an, & n\text{ odd},\\[2mm]
\dfrac b2\,n, & n\text{ even},
\end{cases}
}
\]
with \(a,b\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\).

---

## 5. Check that this form really works

If \(x,y\) have the same parity, then \(x-y\), \(x^2-3y^2\), and \(x^2+y^2\) are all even, so the “even rule” applies to all three terms.

Then
\[
\text{LHS}=\frac b2(x^2-3y^2)+\frac b2(x^2+y^2)=b(x^2-y^2),
\]
and
\[
\text{RHS}=2(x+y)\cdot \frac b2(x-y)=b(x^2-y^2).
\]

If \(x,y\) have opposite parity, then all three arguments are odd, so the “odd rule” applies:
\[
\text{LHS}=a(x^2-3y^2)+a(x^2+y^2)=2a(x^2-y^2),
\]
\[
\text{RHS}=2(x+y)\cdot a(x-y)=2a(x^2-y^2).
\]
So every such pair \((a,b)\) indeed gives a valid function.

---

## 6. Use the perfect square condition

Since \(2015\) is odd,
\[
f(2015)=2015a.
\]
Since \(2016\) is even,
\[
f(2016)=\frac b2\cdot 2016=1008b.
\]
Thus
\[
f(2015)f(2016)=2015\cdot 1008\cdot ab.
\]

Factor:
\[
2015=5\cdot 13\cdot 31,\qquad 1008=2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 7.
\]
So
\[
2015\cdot 1008=2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31.
\]
Its squarefree part is
\[
5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31=14105.
\]

Therefore \(f(2015)f(2016)\) is a perfect square iff
\[
14105\cdot ab
\]
is a perfect square.

So the squarefree part of \(ab\) must be exactly \(14105\). Write
\[
a=d u^2,\qquad b=e v^2,
\]
where \(d,e\) are squarefree. Then the primes \(5,7,13,31\) must be split between \(d\) and \(e\), so
\[
de=14105.
\]
Hence
\[
a+b=du^2+ev^2\ge d+e.
\]
So the minimum occurs when \(u=v=1\), i.e. when \(a\) and \(b\) themselves are complementary factor pairs of \(14105\).

Now factor
\[
14105=5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31.
\]
The factor pair closest together is
\[
91=7\cdot 13,\qquad 155=5\cdot 31,
\]
and
\[
91+155=246.
\]

This is achievable, for example by
\[
a=91,\qquad b=155.
\]

So the minimum value is
\[
\boxed{246}.
\]

\[
\boxed{246}
\]

Let  
\[
P(x,y):\quad f(x^2-3y^2)+f(x^2+y^2)=2(x+y)f(x-y)
\]
for all \(x,y\in\mathbb Z\).

We seek the minimum of \(f(1)+f(2)\).

---

## 1. First basic consequences

### From \(P(0,0)\)
\[
2f(0)=0 \implies f(0)=0.
\]

### From \(P(x,0)\)
\[
f(x^2)+f(x^2)=2x f(x)
\]
so
\[
f(x^2)=x f(x)\qquad(\forall x\in\mathbb Z).
\]

Now replace \(x\) by \(-x\):
\[
f(x^2)=(-x)f(-x).
\]
Comparing with \(f(x^2)=x f(x)\), we get
\[
x f(x)=-x f(-x).
\]
Hence for all \(x\),
\[
f(-x)=-f(x).
\]
So \(f\) is odd.

---

## 2. A key relation

Compare \(P(x,y)\) and \(P(-x,y)\).

Since \((-x)^2=x^2\), the left-hand sides are equal. Thus
\[
2(x+y)f(x-y)=2(-x+y)f(-x-y).
\]
Using oddness \(f(-x-y)=-f(x+y)\),
\[
2(x+y)f(x-y)=2(x-y)f(x+y).
\]
So
\[
(x+y)f(x-y)=(x-y)f(x+y). \tag{1}
\]

Now let
\[
u=x-y,\qquad v=x+y.
\]
Then \(u,v\) have the same parity, and every pair of integers with the same parity arises this way. Thus (1) becomes

\[
v f(u)=u f(v)\qquad\text{for all }u,v\in\mathbb Z\text{ of the same parity}. \tag{2}
\]

---

## 3. Determine all solutions

### Odd inputs
Take \(u=1\) in (2). For every odd \(v\),
\[
v f(1)=f(v).
\]
So for odd \(n\),
\[
f(n)=n f(1).
\]

Let
\[
a:=f(1)\in\mathbb Z_{>0}.
\]
Then
\[
f(n)=an \qquad (n\text{ odd}). \tag{3}
\]

### Even inputs
Take \(u=2\) in (2). For every even \(v\),
\[
v f(2)=2f(v).
\]
So for even \(n\),
\[
f(n)=\frac{n}{2}f(2).
\]

Let
\[
t:=f(2)\in\mathbb Z_{>0}.
\]
Then
\[
f(n)=\frac{tn}{2}\qquad (n\text{ even}). \tag{4}
\]

So every solution must be
\[
f(n)=
\begin{cases}
an,& n\text{ odd},\\[2mm]
\dfrac{tn}{2},& n\text{ even},
\end{cases}
\]
with \(a,t>0\).

Conversely, this form indeed satisfies the FE:
- if \(x,y\) have same parity, then \(x^2-3y^2,\ x^2+y^2,\ x-y\) are all even;
- if \(x,y\) have opposite parity, they are all odd.

In either case the equation reduces to the identity
\[
(x^2-3y^2)+(x^2+y^2)=2(x^2-y^2)=2(x+y)(x-y).
\]
So this is the full family of solutions.

---

## 4. Use the square condition

Since \(2015\) is odd and \(2016\) is even,
\[
f(2015)=2015a,\qquad f(2016)=\frac{2016}{2}t=1008t.
\]
Therefore
\[
f(2015)f(2016)=2015\cdot 1008\cdot a t.
\]

Factor:
\[
2015=5\cdot 13\cdot 31,\qquad 1008=2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 7.
\]
Hence
\[
2015\cdot 1008=2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31.
\]
The squarefree part is
\[
5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31=14105.
\]
So \(f(2015)f(2016)\) is a perfect square iff
\[
at=14105\cdot s^2
\]
for some integer \(s\ge 1\).

To minimize \(a+t\), we clearly want the smallest possible product, so take \(s=1\):
\[
at=14105.
\]

Now factor \(14105\):
\[
14105=5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31.
\]
We want two positive integer factors with minimal sum. Since \(\sqrt{14105}\approx 118.8\), check divisors below this:
\[
1,5,7,13,31,35,65,91.
\]
The closest factor to \(\sqrt{14105}\) is \(91\), giving the pair
\[
91\cdot 155=14105.
\]
Thus the minimum sum is
\[
91+155=246.
\]

This is achievable, for example with
\[
a=91,\qquad t=155.
\]

So the minimum value of \(f(1)+f(2)\) is

\[
\boxed{246}.
\]

Let  
\[
P(x,y):\quad f(x^2-3y^2)+f(x^2+y^2)=2(x+y)f(x-y)
\]
for all integers \(x,y\).

We want the minimum of \(f(1)+f(2)\).

---

## 1. First basic identities

Set \(y=0\) in \(P(x,y)\):
\[
f(x^2)+f(x^2)=2x f(x),
\]
so
\[
f(x^2)=x f(x)\qquad (\forall x\in\mathbb Z). \tag{1}
\]

Now replace \(x\) by \(-x\) in (1):
\[
f(x^2)=-x f(-x).
\]
Comparing with \(f(x^2)=x f(x)\), for \(x\neq 0\),
\[
f(-x)=-f(x). \tag{2}
\]
So \(f\) is odd on nonzero integers.

Also from \(P(0,0)\):
\[
2f(0)=0 \implies f(0)=0.
\]
Thus (2) holds for all integers.

---

## 2. A key recurrence

Compare \(P(x,1)\) and \(P(x,-1)\).

Since both have the same left-hand side,
\[
f(x^2-3)+f(x^2+1),
\]
their right-hand sides must be equal:
\[
2(x+1)f(x-1)=2(x-1)f(x+1).
\]
Hence
\[
(x+1)f(x-1)=(x-1)f(x+1)\qquad (\forall x\in\mathbb Z). \tag{3}
\]

This recurrence links values of the same parity.

---

## 3. Determine all values from \(f(1)\) and \(f(2)\)

Let
\[
a=f(1),\qquad b=f(2).
\]

### Odd positive integers
Use (3) with \(x=2k\):
\[
(2k+1)f(2k-1)=(2k-1)f(2k+1).
\]
Starting from \(f(1)=a\), induction gives
\[
f(2k+1)=(2k+1)a \qquad (k\ge0). \tag{4}
\]

### Even positive integers
Use (3) with \(x=2k+1\):
\[
(2k+2)f(2k)=2k\,f(2k+2).
\]
Starting from \(f(2)=b\), induction gives
\[
f(2k)=k\,b \qquad (k\ge1). \tag{5}
\]

Using oddness (2), this extends to all integers.

So every solution must be of the form
\[
f(n)=
\begin{cases}
an,& n\text{ odd},\\[2mm]
\dfrac b2\,n,& n\text{ even}.
\end{cases}
\tag{6}
\]

Conversely, any such function works, because in \(P(x,y)\), the three inputs
\[
x^2-3y^2,\quad x^2+y^2,\quad x-y
\]
always have the same parity, so both sides reduce to the same linear identity.

Thus the whole problem is now about positive integers \(a,b\).

---

## 4. Use the square condition

Since \(2015\) is odd and \(2016\) is even,
\[
f(2015)=2015a,\qquad f(2016)=1008b.
\]
So
\[
f(2015)f(2016)=2015\cdot 1008\cdot ab
\]
is a perfect square.

Factor:
\[
2015=5\cdot 13\cdot 31,\qquad 1008=2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 7.
\]
Hence
\[
2015\cdot 1008 = 2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31.
\]
The squarefree part is
\[
5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31=14105.
\]
Therefore
\[
ab=14105\,t^2
\]
for some positive integer \(t\).

We want to minimize \(a+b\).

If \(t\ge2\), then
\[
a+b\ge 2\sqrt{ab}=2t\sqrt{14105}>475,
\]
so the minimum cannot occur there.

Thus we must have \(t=1\), so
\[
ab=14105.
\]

Now factor \(14105=5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31\).  
To minimize \(a+b\) with fixed product, choose divisors closest to \(\sqrt{14105}\approx 118.8\).

The closest factor pair is
\[
91\cdot 155 = 14105,
\]
giving
\[
a+b=91+155=246.
\]

This is achievable, e.g.
\[
a=91,\quad b=155.
\]

---

## 5. Check existence

Define
\[
f(n)=
\begin{cases}
91n,& n\text{ odd},\\[2mm]
\frac{155}{2}n,& n\text{ even}.
\end{cases}
\]
This is integer-valued, positive for \(n>0\), satisfies the functional equation, and
\[
f(2015)f(2016)
=(2015\cdot 91)(1008\cdot 155)
\]
is indeed a perfect square.

So the minimum is attained.

\[
\boxed{246}
\]

Let
\[
P(x,y):\quad f(x^2-3y^2)+f(x^2+y^2)=2(x+y)f(x-y)
\]
for all integers \(x,y\).

We want the minimum of \(f(1)+f(2)\).

---

### 1) First basic consequences

Take \(y=0\) in \(P(x,y)\):
\[
f(x^2)+f(x^2)=2x f(x),
\]
so
\[
f(x^2)=x f(x)\qquad (\forall x\in\mathbb Z).
\]

From \(x=0\), we get \(f(0)=0\).

Now compare \(x\) and \(-x\):
\[
f(x^2)=x f(x)=(-x)f(-x),
\]
hence for all \(x\),
\[
f(-x)=-f(x).
\]
So \(f\) is odd.

---

### 2) Form of the function

Let
\[
a=f(1),\qquad b=f(2).
\]
Since \(f(n)>0\) for \(n>0\), we have \(a,b>0\).

A parity check in the functional equation shows:

- If \(x,y\) have opposite parity, then
  \[
  x^2-3y^2,\; x^2+y^2,\; x-y
  \]
  are all odd.

- If \(x,y\) have the same parity, then those three numbers are all even.

Using this together with \(f(x^2)=x f(x)\), one gets that the quotient \(f(n)/n\) is constant on odd integers and constant on even integers. Therefore
\[
f(n)=an \quad (n\text{ odd}),
\]
and
\[
f(n)=\frac b2\,n \quad (n\text{ even}).
\]

Conversely, any function of this form works:

- if \(x,y\) are of opposite parity, both sides become
  \[
  a\big((x^2-3y^2)+(x^2+y^2)\big)=2a(x^2-y^2)=2(x+y)a(x-y);
  \]

- if \(x,y\) are of the same parity, both sides become
  \[
  \frac b2\big((x^2-3y^2)+(x^2+y^2)\big)=b(x^2-y^2)
  =2(x+y)\frac b2(x-y).
  \]

So the full family is exactly
\[
f(n)=
\begin{cases}
an,& n\text{ odd},\\[2mm]
\frac b2\,n,& n\text{ even},
\end{cases}
\qquad a,b\in \mathbb Z_{>0}.
\]

Thus
\[
f(1)=a,\qquad f(2)=b.
\]

---

### 3) Use the square condition

Now
\[
f(2015)=2015a
\]
since \(2015\) is odd, and
\[
f(2016)=\frac b2\cdot 2016 = 1008b
\]
since \(2016\) is even.

Hence
\[
f(2015)f(2016)=2015\cdot 1008\cdot ab.
\]

Factor:
\[
2015=5\cdot 13\cdot 31,\qquad 1008=2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 7.
\]
So
\[
2015\cdot 1008 = 2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31.
\]

For this product times \(ab\) to be a perfect square, \(ab\) must contribute exactly the odd-prime part
\[
5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31 = 14105
\]
up to a square factor. Thus
\[
ab = 14105\cdot t^2
\]
for some positive integer \(t\).

To minimize \(a+b\), we clearly want the smallest possible product, so take \(t=1\):
\[
ab=14105.
\]

Now factor
\[
14105=5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31.
\]
Among divisor pairs, the closest pair is
\[
91\cdot 155=14105,
\]
giving
\[
a+b=91+155=246.
\]

This is attainable, for example with
\[
a=91,\quad b=155.
\]

Then indeed
\[
f(2015)f(2016)=2015\cdot 91\cdot 1008\cdot 155
=(2^2\cdot 3\cdot 5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31)^2,
\]
a perfect square.

Therefore the minimum value of \(f(1)+f(2)\) is

\[
\boxed{246}.
\]

Let \(n=2022\) and \(r=800\).

We seek the smallest \(m\) such that every family \(\mathcal F\) of distinct subsets of \(X=\{1,2,\dots,2022\}\) satisfying

- \(|F|\ge 800\) for all \(F\in\mathcal F\),
- every \(x\in X\) belongs to at least \(800\) sets of \(\mathcal F\),

must contain \(m\) sets whose union is \(X\).

We will prove the answer is \(1222\).

---

## 1. Lower bound: \(m\ge 1222\)

We construct a family for which no \(1221\) sets cover \(X\).

Split \(X\) into two parts:

- \(Y=\{y_1,y_2,\dots,y_{1222}\}\),
- \(Z=\{z_1,z_2,\dots,z_{800}\}\).

So \(|Y|=1222\), \(|Z|=800\), and \(X=Y\sqcup Z\).

For each \(i\in\{1,\dots,1222\}\), define \(801\) sets:

- \(F_{i,*}=\{y_i\}\cup Z\),
- for each \(j=1,\dots,800\),
  \[
  F_{i,j}=\{y_i\}\cup (Z\setminus\{z_j\}).
  \]

Each such set has size \(801\) or \(800\), so the size condition holds.

### Check the degree condition
- Each \(y_i\) belongs to exactly the \(801\) sets of type \(i\), so in particular to at least \(800\) sets.
- Each \(z_j\in Z\) belongs, for each fixed \(i\), to all sets of type \(i\) except \(F_{i,j}\), i.e. to \(800\) such sets. Hence \(z_j\) belongs to
  \[
  1222\cdot 800
  \]
  sets, certainly at least \(800\).

So all conditions are satisfied.

### Why \(1221\) sets cannot cover \(X\)
Every set in this family contains exactly one element of \(Y\). Therefore the union of any \(1221\) sets can contain at most \(1221\) elements of \(Y\), so at least one \(y_i\) is missing.

Thus no \(1221\) sets cover \(X\).

### But \(1222\) sets can
Take \(F_{1,*},F_{2,*},\dots,F_{1222,*}\). Their union is \(Y\cup Z=X\).

So this family has covering number \(1222\). Hence
\[
m\ge 1222.
\]

---

## 2. Upper bound: \(m\le 1222\)

Now take any family \(\mathcal F\) satisfying the two given conditions. We show some \(1222\) sets in \(\mathcal F\) cover \(X\).

Let \(t\) be the minimum number of sets from \(\mathcal F\) whose union is \(X\). Choose such a minimal cover:
\[
C_1,C_2,\dots,C_t.
\]

Because this cover is minimal, each \(C_i\) contains an element \(y_i\) that is not contained in any other \(C_j\) (\(j\ne i\)). Such an element is called a private element.

So \(y_1,\dots,y_t\) are distinct. Let
\[
Y=\{y_1,\dots,y_t\},\qquad Z=X\setminus Y.
\]

Since \(y_j\notin C_i\) for \(j\ne i\), each \(C_i\) contains exactly one element of \(Y\), namely \(y_i\). Therefore
\[
C_i\setminus\{y_i\}\subseteq Z.
\]

But \(|C_i|\ge 800\), so
\[
|Z|\ge 799.
\]
Hence
\[
t=|Y|=2022-|Z|\le 2022-799=1223.
\]

So any minimal cover has size at most \(1223\). We now rule out the case \(t=1223\).

---

### Suppose \(t=1223\)

Then \(|Z|=799\). Since \(C_i\subseteq Z\cup\{y_i\}\) and \(|C_i|\ge 800\), it follows that necessarily
\[
C_i=Z\cup\{y_i\}
\]
for every \(i\).

Now fix some \(i\). The element \(y_i\) belongs to at least \(800\) sets of \(\mathcal F\), but among the chosen cover sets \(C_1,\dots,C_{1223}\), only \(C_i\) contains \(y_i\). So there exists some other set \(G\in\mathcal F\), \(G\ne C_i\), with \(y_i\in G\).

Since \(|G|\ge 800\) and \(|Z|=799\), the set \(G\) must contain some element outside \(Z\), i.e. some \(y_j\). Because \(G\ne C_i\), this \(y_j\) must satisfy \(j\ne i\).

Now consider the \(1222\) sets:
\[
G,\; C_k\ (k\ne i,j).
\]
These cover all of \(X\):

- \(G\) covers \(y_i\) and \(y_j\),
- each other \(y_k\) is covered by \(C_k\),
- every element of \(Z\) is covered by every \(C_k\).

So \(1222\) sets cover \(X\), contradicting the minimality of \(t=1223\).

Therefore \(t\ne 1223\), and hence
\[
t\le 1222.
\]

So every such family has \(1222\) sets whose union is \(X\). Therefore
\[
m\le 1222.
\]

---

## 3. Conclusion

From the construction, \(m\ge 1222\).  
From the general argument, \(m\le 1222\).

Hence the smallest such integer is

\[
\boxed{1222}.
\]

Let \(n=2022\). We want the smallest \(m\) such that every family \(\mathcal F\) satisfying

- \(|F|\ge 800\) for all \(F\in\mathcal F\),
- every \(x\in X\) belongs to at least \(800\) sets of \(\mathcal F\),

must contain \(m\) sets whose union is \(X\).

We will show:

- \(m\ge 1222\) by construction,
- \(m\le 1222\) by a general argument.

So the answer is \(1222\).

---

## 1. Lower bound: \(m\ge 1222\)

We construct a family for which **no \(1221\) sets** cover \(X\).

Let
\[
X=R\sqcup \{a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{1222}\},
\]
where \(|R|=800\).

For each \(i\in\{1,\dots,1222\}\) and each \(r\in R\), define
\[
F_{i,r}=\{a_i\}\cup (R\setminus\{r\}).
\]
Each such set has size
\[
1+(800-1)=800.
\]

Let
\[
\mathcal F=\{F_{i,r}:1\le i\le 1222,\ r\in R\}.
\]

### Check the conditions
- Every set has size \(800\).
- Each \(a_i\) lies in exactly \(800\) sets: namely \(F_{i,r}\) for \(r\in R\).
- Each \(r\in R\) lies in all sets except those with that same omitted element \(r\), so it lies in
\[
1222\cdot 799 \ge 800
\]
sets.

Thus \(\mathcal F\) satisfies the problem conditions.

### Why \(1221\) sets cannot cover \(X\)
Each set \(F_{i,r}\) contains exactly one of the elements \(a_1,\dots,a_{1222}\). Hence \(1221\) sets can cover at most \(1221\) of these \(1222\) elements. So they cannot cover \(X\).

Therefore, the required \(m\) must satisfy
\[
m\ge 1222.
\]

---

## 2. Upper bound: \(m\le 1222\)

Now let \(\mathcal F\) be any family satisfying the conditions. Let \(k\) be the minimum number of sets from \(\mathcal F\) whose union is \(X\). We show \(k\le 1222\).

Take a minimal cover
\[
F_1,F_2,\dots,F_k.
\]

Because the cover is minimal, each \(F_i\) has a **private element** \(x_i\in F_i\) that does not belong to any other \(F_j\) (\(j\ne i\)). So the \(x_i\)'s are distinct.

Let
\[
P=\{x_1,\dots,x_k\},\qquad Q=X\setminus P.
\]
Then \(|Q|=2022-k\).

Now note: since \(x_j\notin F_i\) for \(j\ne i\), each \(F_i\) contains **exactly one** element of \(P\), namely \(x_i\). Since \(|F_i|\ge 800\), each \(F_i\) must contain at least \(799\) elements of \(Q\). Therefore
\[
|Q|\ge 799.
\]
So
\[
2022-k\ge 799 \implies k\le 1223.
\]

Thus only \(k=1223\) remains to be ruled out.

### Suppose \(k=1223\)
Then \(|Q|=2022-1223=799\). Since each \(F_i\) contains at least \(799\) elements of \(Q\), it must contain **all** of \(Q\). Hence
\[
F_i=Q\cup\{x_i\}\qquad (1\le i\le 1223).
\]

Now consider any set \(G\in\mathcal F\).

- If \(G\) contained two of the private elements, say \(x_i,x_j\), then
  \[
  G,\ F_1,\dots,\widehat{F_i},\dots,\widehat{F_j},\dots,F_{1223}
  \]
  would be a cover of size \(1222\), contradiction.

So **no set in \(\mathcal F\) contains two elements of \(P\)**.

But \(|Q|=799\), and every set in \(\mathcal F\) has size at least \(800\). Therefore every set must contain at least one element of \(P\). Combined with the previous statement, every set contains **exactly one** element of \(P\), and hence must be of the form
\[
Q\cup\{x_i\}.
\]
So there is only one set containing \(x_i\), namely \(Q\cup\{x_i\}\).

That contradicts the condition that each element of \(X\), in particular each \(x_i\), belongs to at least \(800\) sets of \(\mathcal F\).

Hence \(k\neq 1223\). Therefore
\[
k\le 1222.
\]

So any such family always has \(1222\) sets whose union is \(X\).

---

Combining the lower and upper bounds:
\[
m=1222.
\]

\[
\boxed{1222}

Let \(n=2022\), and let us call a subfamily of \(\mathcal F\) a **cover** if its union is \(X\).

We will show:

1. There is always a cover using at most \(1222\) sets.
2. There exists a family \(\mathcal F\) for which no \(1221\) sets cover \(X\).

So the answer is \(1222\).

---

## Step 1: An upper bound

Take a cover of \(X\) with the **smallest possible number** of sets:
\[
F_0,F_1,\dots,F_{t-1}.
\]
Because it is minimal, each \(F_i\) has a **private element** \(a_i\in F_i\) not belonging to any other \(F_j\).

### First bound: \(t\le 1223\)

For \(i\ge 1\), the private element \(a_i\notin F_0\). Hence
\[
a_1,\dots,a_{t-1}\in X\setminus F_0.
\]
These are distinct, so
\[
t-1\le |X\setminus F_0| \le 2022-800=1222.
\]
Therefore
\[
t\le 1223.
\]

So any minimal cover has size at most \(1223\).  
We now rule out the case \(t=1223\).

---

## Step 2: Show \(t\neq 1223\)

Assume for contradiction that \(t=1223\).

Then from
\[
t-1\le |X\setminus F_0|\le 1222
\]
we must have equality throughout, so:

- \(|F_0|=800\),
- \(|X\setminus F_0|=1222\),
- and the private elements \(a_1,\dots,a_{1222}\) are exactly all elements of \(X\setminus F_0\).

Now fix \(i\ge 1\). Since every element outside \(F_0\) is one of the \(a_j\), and \(a_j\) is private to \(F_j\), no set \(F_i\) can contain any \(a_j\) with \(j\ne i\). Thus
\[
F_i\subseteq F_0\cup\{a_i\}.
\]

Also, \(a_0\) is private to \(F_0\), so \(a_0\notin F_i\) for all \(i\ge 1\). Hence
\[
F_i\subseteq (F_0\setminus\{a_0\})\cup\{a_i\}.
\]
The right-hand side has size \(799+1=800\), while every \(F_i\) has size at least \(800\). Therefore
\[
F_i=(F_0\setminus\{a_0\})\cup\{a_i\}\qquad (i=1,\dots,1222).
\]

Now use the degree condition on the element \(a_0\): it belongs to at least \(800\) sets of \(\mathcal F\). One of them is \(F_0\), so there exists some other set \(G\ne F_0\) containing \(a_0\).

Because \(|G|\ge 800\) and \(|F_0|=800\), \(G\) cannot be a proper subset of \(F_0\); since \(G\ne F_0\), it must contain some element outside \(F_0\), say \(a_i\).

Now consider the \(1222\) sets
\[
G,\ F_1,\dots,F_{i-1},F_{i+1},\dots,F_{1222}.
\]
They cover \(X\), because:

- \(G\) contains \(a_0\) and \(a_i\),
- the other \(F_j\)'s cover all \(a_j\) for \(j\ne i\),
- and every \(F_j\) (\(j\ge1\)) contains all of \(F_0\setminus\{a_0\}\).

So we found a cover with \(1222\) sets, contradicting minimality of \(t=1223\).

Therefore \(t\le 1222\).

So **every** such family \(\mathcal F\) has a cover of size at most \(1222\).

---

## Step 3: A construction showing \(1221\) may fail

We now build a family for which at least \(1222\) sets are necessary.

Let
\[
X=C\sqcup U,
\]
where

- \(C\) is a set of \(800\) elements,
- \(U=\{u_1,u_2,\dots,u_{1222}\}\).

For each \(i\in\{1,\dots,1222\}\) and each \(c\in C\), define
\[
F_{i,c}=\{u_i\}\cup (C\setminus\{c\}).
\]
Let
\[
\mathcal F=\{F_{i,c}:1\le i\le 1222,\ c\in C\}.
\]

### Check the conditions

- Each set has size
  \[
  1+(800-1)=800.
  \]
- Each \(u_i\) belongs to exactly \(800\) sets \(F_{i,c}\).
- Each \(x\in C\) belongs to all sets \(F_{i,c}\) except those with \(c=x\), so it belongs to
  \[
  1222\cdot 799 \ge 800
  \]
  sets.

So the family satisfies all requirements.

### Why \(1221\) sets cannot cover \(X\)

Each set \(F_{i,c}\) contains exactly one element of \(U\), namely \(u_i\). Therefore the union of \(1221\) sets can contain at most \(1221\) elements of \(U\), so at least one \(u_i\) is missed.

Hence no \(1221\) sets cover \(X\).

### Why \(1222\) sets can cover \(X\)

Choose one set for each \(i\), say \(F_{i,c_i}\), with the omitted elements \(c_i\) not all the same (for example, for the first \(800\) indices choose all different \(c_i\), and arbitrary thereafter). Then all \(u_i\)'s are covered, and every element of \(C\) is included in at least one chosen set. So these \(1222\) sets cover \(X\).

Thus this family has cover number exactly \(1222\).

---

Since \(1222\) is always sufficient, and sometimes necessary, the smallest such integer is

\[
\boxed{1222}.

- every \(x\in X\) lies in at least \(800\) sets of \(\mathcal F\),

must contain \(m\) sets whose union is \(X\).

We will prove:

- \(m\ge 1222\) by construction;
- \(m\le 1222\) by a general argument.

So the answer will be \(1222\).

## 1. Lower bound: \(m\ge 1222\)

Split \(X\) into two parts:

- \(A=\{1,2,\dots,800\}\), so \(|A|=800\),
- \(B=X\setminus A\), so \(|B|=1222\).

For each \(x\in B\) and each \(a\in A\), define
\[
F_{x,a}=(A\setminus\{a\})\cup\{x\}.
\]
Each such set has size
\[
|F_{x,a}|=799+1=800.
\]
Let
\[
\mathcal F=\{F_{x,a}: x\in B,\ a\in A\}.
\]

### Check the conditions
- All sets are distinct.
- Every set has size \(800\).
- For each \(x\in B\), \(x\) belongs to exactly the \(800\) sets \(F_{x,a}\) as \(a\) varies in \(A\).
- For each \(a\in A\), \(a\) belongs to all \(F_{x,b}\) with \(b\ne a\), so it belongs to
\[
1222\cdot 799
\]
sets, certainly at least \(800\).

So \(\mathcal F\) satisfies the problem conditions.

### Why \(1221\) sets cannot cover \(X\)
Each set \(F_{x,a}\) contains exactly **one** element of \(B\). Hence the union of any \(1221\) sets contains at most \(1221\) elements of \(B\). But \(|B|=1222\). So no \(1221\) sets can cover \(X\).

Thus
\[
m\ge 1222.
\]

## 2. Upper bound: \(m\le 1222\)

Now take any family \(\mathcal F\) satisfying the conditions.

Let \(t\) be the minimum number of sets from \(\mathcal F\) whose union is \(X\). Choose such a minimum cover:
\[
F_1,F_2,\dots,F_t.
\]

Because this cover is minimal, each \(F_i\) has a **private element** \(x_i\in F_i\) that lies in no other \(F_j\) (\(j\ne i\)). So the \(x_i\)'s are distinct.

### First bound: \(t\le 1223\)
Fix \(i\). Since \(x_j\notin F_i\) for every \(j\ne i\), the complement \(X\setminus F_i\) contains at least the \(t-1\) elements
\[
x_1,\dots,x_{i-1},x_{i+1},\dots,x_t.
\]
But \(|F_i|\ge 800\), so
\[
|X\setminus F_i|\le 2022-800=1222.
\]
Hence
\[
t-1\le 1222 \quad\Rightarrow\quad t\le 1223.
\]

So a minimum cover has size at most \(1223\).

### Now rule out \(t=1223\)
Assume for contradiction that \(t=1223\).

Then for each \(i\), \(X\setminus F_i\) contains the other \(1222\) private elements. Since \(|X\setminus F_i|\le 1222\), it follows that
\[
X\setminus F_i=\{x_j: j\ne i\}.
\]
So each \(F_i\) must be exactly
\[
F_i = Y\cup\{x_i\},
\]
where
\[
Y = X\setminus\{x_1,\dots,x_{1223}\}.
\]
Since \(2022-1223=799\), we have \(|Y|=799\).

Now look at \(x_1\). By hypothesis, \(x_1\) belongs to at least \(800\) sets of \(\mathcal F\). One of them is \(F_1\), so there is some other set \(G\in\mathcal F\), \(G\ne F_1\), with \(x_1\in G\).

Because \(|G|\ge 800\) and \(Y\) has only \(799\) elements, \(G\) must contain some element outside \(Y\), i.e. some \(x_j\) with \(j\ne 1\).

So \(G\) contains both \(x_1\) and \(x_j\) for some \(j\ne 1\).

But then the \(1222\) sets
\[
G,\ F_2,\dots,F_{j-1},F_{j+1},\dots,F_{1223}
\]
cover \(X\):

- \(G\) covers \(x_1\) and \(x_j\),
- each other \(x_k\) is covered by \(F_k\),
- every element of \(Y\) is covered by all the \(F_k\)'s.

This contradicts the minimality of \(t=1223\).

Therefore \(t\neq 1223\), so
\[
t\le 1222.
\]

Thus in every such family, there exist \(1222\) sets whose union is \(X\). Hence
\[
m\le 1222.
\]

---

## 3. Conclusion

We proved both
\[
m\ge 1222
\quad\text{and}\quad
m\le 1222.
\]
Therefore
\[
m=1222.
\]

Then:

- all these sets are distinct.

### Check the conditions
- If \(p\in P\), then \(p\) belongs exactly to the \(800\) sets \(F_{p,y}\) (\(y\in Y\)).
- If \(y\in Y\), then for each \(p\in P\), the element \(y\) is missing from exactly one set \(F_{p,y}\), so it belongs to
  \[

  \]
  sets.

So the given conditions are satisfied.

### Why \(1221\) sets cannot cover \(X\)
Each set \(F_{p,y}\) contains exactly one element of \(P\). Therefore the union of any \(1221\) such sets contains at most \(1221\) elements of \(P\), so at least one element of \(P\) is missing.

## 2. Upper bound: every family can be covered by \(1222\) sets

Take a subfamily
\[
F_1,F_2,\dots,F_t\in\mathcal F
\]
whose union is \(X\), with \(t\) minimal.

We will prove \(t\le 1222\).

### Step 1: Minimality gives private elements
Because the cover is minimal, for each \(i\) there exists an element
\[
x_i\in F_i\setminus \bigcup_{j\ne i} F_j.
\]
So \(x_i\) belongs to \(F_i\) and to none of the other selected sets.

These \(x_i\)'s are distinct.

### Step 2: First bound \(t\le 1223\)
Fix \(i\). Since \(x_j\notin F_i\) for all \(j\ne i\), the set \(F_i\) misses at least \(t-1\) elements, namely \(x_j\) for \(j\ne i\). Hence
\[
2022-|F_i|\ge t-1.
\]
But \(|F_i|\ge 800\), so \(2022-|F_i|\le 1222\). Therefore
\[
t-1\le 1222 \quad\Rightarrow\quad t\le 1223.
\]

So if \(t>1222\), then necessarily
\[
t=1223.
\]

### Step 3: Analyze the extremal case \(t=1223\)
Assume for contradiction that \(t=1223\).

Then for each \(i\), since \(F_i\) misses at least \(1222\) elements and can miss at most \(1222\) elements, it must miss exactly the \(1222\) elements
\[
x_j\quad (j\ne i).
\]
Thus
\[
X\setminus F_i=\{x_j:j\ne i\}.
\]

Let
\[
Y=X\setminus\{x_1,\dots,x_{1223}\}.
\]
Since \(2022-1223=799\), we have \(|Y|=799\). Hence each selected set is exactly
\[
F_i=Y\cup\{x_i\}.
\]

### Step 4: No set in \(\mathcal F\) can contain two of the \(x_i\)'s
Suppose some \(G\in\mathcal F\) contains \(x_i\) and \(x_j\) with \(i\ne j\).

Then
\[
G,\ F_k\ (k\ne i,j)
\]
is a collection of \(1222\) sets.

- The sets \(F_k\) for \(k\ne i,j\) cover all of \(Y\) and all \(x_k\) (\(k\ne i,j\)).
- The set \(G\) covers \(x_i,x_j\).

So these \(1222\) sets cover \(X\), contradicting the minimality of \(t=1223\).

Therefore **every** set in \(\mathcal F\) contains at most one of the elements \(x_1,\dots,x_{1223}\).

### Step 5: Contradiction with the “at least \(800\) sets” condition
Take some \(x_i\). Any set containing \(x_i\) contains no other \(x_j\), and must have size at least \(800\). Since there are only \(799\) elements in \(Y\), such a set must contain all of \(Y\). So the only possible set containing \(x_i\) is
\[
Y\cup\{x_i\}=F_i.
\]
Thus \(x_i\) belongs to only one set of \(\mathcal F\), contradicting the hypothesis that every element belongs to at least \(800\) sets.

So \(t=1223\) is impossible. Hence
\[
t\le 1222.
\]

Therefore every such family has \(1222\) sets whose union is \(X\), so
\[
m\le 1222.
\]

m=1222.

- there is a family for which **1221 sets are not enough**, so \(m\ge 1222\);
- in every such family, **1222 sets are enough**, so \(m\le 1222\).

## 1. Lower bound: construct a family needing at least \(1222\) sets

Split \(X\) into two parts:

- a set \(S\) of size \(800\),
- a set \(U\) of size \(1222\).

So \(X=S\sqcup U\), with \(|S|=800,\ |U|=1222\).

For each \(u\in U\), and for each \(s\in S\), define
\[
F_{u,s}=(S\setminus\{s\})\cup\{u\}.
\]

So for each fixed \(u\), there are \(800\) distinct sets \(F_{u,s}\).

### Check the conditions
- Each \(F_{u,s}\) has size
  \[
  |S|-1+1=800.
  \]
- For any \(u\in U\), the element \(u\) belongs to exactly those \(800\) sets \(F_{u,s}\), so it lies in at least \(800\) sets.
- For any \(x\in S\), for each fixed \(u\in U\), the element \(x\) is omitted only from \(F_{u,x}\), hence appears in \(799\) of the \(800\) sets of type \(u\). So overall it belongs to
  \[
  1222\cdot 799>800
  \]
  sets.

Thus the family satisfies the hypotheses.

### Why at least \(1222\) sets are needed
Each element \(u\in U\) appears **only** in the \(800\) sets indexed by that same \(u\). Therefore, to cover all \(1222\) elements of \(U\), any covering subfamily must contain at least one set for each \(u\in U\). Hence any cover needs at least \(1222\) sets.

So
\[
m\ge 1222.
\]

---

## 2. Upper bound: any such family has a cover of size at most \(1222\)

Let \(\mathcal F\) be any family satisfying the conditions.

Take a subfamily
\[
\{C_1,C_2,\dots,C_q\}\subseteq \mathcal F
\]
that covers \(X\), with \(q\) as small as possible.

We will show \(q\le 1222\).

---

### Step 1: choose private elements

Because the cover is minimal, for each \(i\) there exists an element
\[
x_i\in C_i\setminus \bigcup_{j\ne i} C_j.
\]
So \(x_i\) is covered only by \(C_i\) among these \(q\) sets.

Let
\[
B_i=X\setminus C_i.
\]
Then:

- \(x_i\notin B_i\),
- for \(i\ne j\), since \(x_i\notin C_j\), we have \(x_i\in B_j\).

---

### Step 2: a probability inequality

Take a random permutation of \(X\). For each \(i\), let \(E_i\) be the event that \(x_i\) appears before every element of \(B_i\).

Since among the \(|B_i|+1\) elements of \(\{x_i\}\cup B_i\), each is equally likely to come first,
\[
\Pr(E_i)=\frac1{|B_i|+1}.
\]

Also the events \(E_i\) are pairwise disjoint: if \(i\ne j\), then \(x_i\in B_j\) and \(x_j\in B_i\), so \(E_i\) would force \(x_i\) before \(x_j\), while \(E_j\) would force \(x_j\) before \(x_i\), impossible.

Therefore
\[
\sum_{i=1}^q \frac1{|B_i|+1}\le 1.
\]

Now each \(C_i\) has size at least \(800\), so
\[
|B_i|=2022-|C_i|\le 2022-800=1222.
\]
Hence
\[
\frac1{|B_i|+1}\ge \frac1{1223}.
\]
So
\[
\frac q{1223}\le 1,
\]
which gives
\[
q\le 1223.
\]

---

### Step 3: rule out the case \(q=1223\)

Assume for contradiction that \(q=1223\).

Then from
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{1223}\frac1{|B_i|+1}\le 1
\]
and each term being at least \(1/1223\), every term must actually equal \(1/1223\). So for every \(i\),
\[
|B_i|=1222,
\]
hence
\[
|C_i|=2022-1222=800.
\]

Now the \(1223\) elements \(x_1,\dots,x_{1223}\) are distinct, so there are exactly
\[
2022-1223=799
\]
other elements of \(X\); call that set \(Y\).

Since \(x_j\notin C_i\) for \(j\ne i\), the set \(C_i\) can contain no \(x_j\) except \(x_i\). But \(|C_i|=800\), so besides \(x_i\), it must contain all \(799\) elements of \(Y\). Therefore
\[
C_i=Y\cup\{x_i\}\qquad (1\le i\le 1223).
\]

Now consider any set \(F\in\mathcal F\).

- If \(F\) contained two of the \(x_i\)'s, say \(x_a,x_b\), then
  \[
  F,\ C_1,\dots,\widehat{C_a},\dots,\widehat{C_b},\dots,C_{1223}
  \]
  would be a cover of size \(1222\): the remaining \(C_i\)'s cover all of \(Y\) and all other \(x_i\), while \(F\) covers \(x_a,x_b\). This contradicts minimality of \(q=1223\).

So **every set in \(\mathcal F\) contains at most one of the \(x_i\)**.

But \(Y\) has only \(799\) elements, and every set in \(\mathcal F\) has size at least \(800\). Therefore every set in \(\mathcal F\) must contain **at least one** of the \(x_i\). Combining with “at most one”, every set contains **exactly one** of the \(x_i\).

Then to reach size at least \(800\), such a set must contain all \(799\) elements of \(Y\). So the only possible set containing \(x_i\) is exactly
\[
Y\cup\{x_i\}=C_i.
\]
Thus \(x_i\) belongs to only one set of \(\mathcal F\), contradicting the condition that every element belongs to at least \(800\) sets.

Therefore \(q\neq 1223\). Hence
\[
q\le 1222.
\]

So every such family has \(1222\) sets whose union is \(X\). Therefore
\[
m\le 1222.
\]

---

## Conclusion

We proved
\[
m\ge 1222 \quad\text{and}\quad m\le 1222,
\]
so the smallest possible \(m\) is

\[
\boxed{1222}.
\]

We will show that the required minimum guaranteed number is

\[
m=2022-800=1222.
\]

So we need:

1. a construction showing that \(1221\) sets may fail to cover \(X\), and  
2. a proof that \(1222\) sets always suffice.

---

## 1. Lower bound: \(m\ge 1222\)

Let

\[
A=\{1,2,\dots,800\},\qquad B=\{801,802,\dots,2022\}.
\]

So \(|A|=800\) and \(|B|=1222\).

For each \(a\in A\) and \(b\in B\), define

\[
F_{a,b}=(A\setminus\{a\})\cup \{b\}.
\]

Let

\[
\mathcal F=\{F_{a,b}: a\in A,\ b\in B\}.
\]

### Check the conditions
- Each \(F_{a,b}\) has size \(799+1=800\).
- For any \(b\in B\), the element \(b\) belongs to exactly the \(800\) sets \(F_{a,b}\) (\(a\in A\)).
- For any \(a_0\in A\), the element \(a_0\) belongs to all \(F_{a,b}\) with \(a\neq a_0\), so it belongs to
  \[
  799\cdot 1222>800
  \]
  sets.

Thus the family satisfies the problem conditions.

### Why at least \(1222\) sets are needed
Each set \(F_{a,b}\) contains exactly one element of \(B\). Therefore, to cover all \(1222\) elements of \(B\), we need at least \(1222\) sets.

So no number smaller than \(1222\) can be guaranteed.

Hence,

\[
m\ge 1222.
\]

---

## 2. Upper bound: \(m\le 1222\)

Now take any family \(\mathcal F\) satisfying the conditions, and let \(m\) be the minimum number of sets from \(\mathcal F\) whose union is \(X\).

Choose such a minimum cover:

\[
F_1,F_2,\dots,F_m.
\]

Because this cover is minimal, each \(F_i\) has a **private element** \(x_i\), meaning

\[
x_i\in F_i,\qquad x_i\notin \bigcup_{j\ne i}F_j.
\]

These \(x_i\)'s are distinct. Let

\[
P=\{x_1,x_2,\dots,x_m\}.
\]

Since \(x_j\notin F_i\) for \(j\ne i\), each \(F_i\) contains no private element except \(x_i\). Thus

\[
F_i\subseteq (X\setminus P)\cup\{x_i\}.
\]

So

\[
|F_i|\le |X\setminus P|+1=(2022-m)+1=2023-m.
\]

But every \(F_i\) has size at least \(800\). Therefore

\[
2023-m\ge 800,
\]
so

\[
m\le 1223.
\]

So the only possible obstruction is \(m=1223\). We now rule that out.

---

### Suppose \(m=1223\)

Then \(|X\setminus P|=2022-1223=799\). Since each \(F_i\subseteq (X\setminus P)\cup\{x_i\}\), we get

\[
|F_i|\le 799+1=800.
\]

But also \(|F_i|\ge 800\), so in fact every \(F_i\) has size exactly \(800\), and

\[
F_i=(X\setminus P)\cup\{x_i\}.
\]

Now fix \(i\). The element \(x_i\) belongs to at least \(800\) sets in \(\mathcal F\). One of them is \(F_i\). Therefore there is some other set \(G\in\mathcal F\) with \(x_i\in G\).

If \(G\) contained no other private element \(x_j\) (\(j\ne i\)), then

\[
G\subseteq (X\setminus P)\cup\{x_i\},
\]
whose size is exactly \(800\). Since \(|G|\ge 800\), this would force

\[
G=(X\setminus P)\cup\{x_i\}=F_i,
\]
contradicting that all sets are distinct and \(G\ne F_i\).

So every other set containing \(x_i\) must also contain some \(x_j\) with \(j\ne i\).

Hence there exists a set \(G\in\mathcal F\) containing both \(x_i\) and \(x_j\) for some \(j\ne i\).

Now replace \(F_i\) and \(F_j\) by \(G\). The sets

\[
G,\quad F_k\ (k\ne i,j)
\]

are only \(1222\) sets, and they still cover:
- all elements of \(X\setminus P\), because every \(F_k\) contains all of \(X\setminus P\);
- all private elements \(x_k\) for \(k\ne i,j\), via \(F_k\);
- \(x_i\) and \(x_j\), via \(G\).

So these \(1222\) sets cover \(X\), contradicting the minimality of \(m=1223\).

Therefore \(m\neq 1223\), and thus

\[
m\le 1222.
\]

---

## Conclusion

We proved
\[
m\ge 1222
\quad\text{and}\quad
m\le 1222.
\]

Therefore the smallest such integer is

\[
\boxed{1222}.
\]

Let \(t\) be the minimum number of sets in \(\mathcal F\) whose union is \(X\).  
We want the largest possible value of \(t\) under the given conditions.

We will show:

1. Always \(t\le 1222\).
2. There is an example with \(t=1222\).

So the answer is \(1222\).

---

## Step 1: A general upper bound

Take a minimum cover
\[
F_1,F_2,\dots,F_t\in\mathcal F,\qquad F_1\cup\cdots\cup F_t=X.
\]
Since this cover is minimal, each \(F_i\) has a **private element** \(x_i\), meaning
\[
x_i\in F_i,\qquad x_i\notin F_j\ \ (j\ne i).
\]
Let
\[
P=\{x_1,x_2,\dots,x_t\}.
\]
Then for each \(i\),
\[
F_i\cap P=\{x_i\},
\]
so \(F_i\) contains at most one element of \(P\).

But every \(F_i\) has size at least \(800\). Hence the other \(799\) elements of \(F_i\) must come from \(X\setminus P\). Therefore
\[
|X\setminus P|\ge 799.
\]
Since \(|X|=2022\) and \(|P|=t\),
\[
2022-t\ge 799 \implies t\le 1223.
\]

So a minimum cover can never need more than \(1223\) sets.

---

## Step 2: Rule out \(t=1223\)

Assume for contradiction that \(t=1223\).

Then
\[
|X\setminus P|=2022-1223=799.
\]
Each \(F_i\) contains exactly one element of \(P\), namely \(x_i\), and has size at least \(800\). Since only \(799\) elements lie outside \(P\), it follows that each \(F_i\) must contain **all** of \(X\setminus P\). Thus
\[
F_i=(X\setminus P)\cup\{x_i\}\qquad (1\le i\le 1223).
\]

Now claim: **no set in \(\mathcal F\) can contain two elements of \(P\).**

Indeed, suppose some \(F\in\mathcal F\) contains \(x_i\) and \(x_j\) with \(i\ne j\).  
Then replacing \(F_i\) and \(F_j\) by \(F\), the sets
\[
F,\ F_1,\dots,\widehat{F_i},\dots,\widehat{F_j},\dots,F_t
\]
would still cover \(X\):

- \(x_i,x_j\) are covered by \(F\),
- every other \(x_k\) is covered by \(F_k\),
- every element of \(X\setminus P\) is covered by any remaining \(F_k\), since each \(F_k\) contains all of \(X\setminus P\).

So \(X\) would be covered by only \(1222\) sets, contradicting the minimality of \(t=1223\).

Hence every set in \(\mathcal F\) contains **at most one** element of \(P\).

But now fix \(x_i\in P\). Any set containing \(x_i\) cannot contain any other element of \(P\), so to have size at least \(800\), it must contain all \(799\) elements of \(X\setminus P\). Therefore the **only** set in \(\mathcal F\) containing \(x_i\) is
\[
(X\setminus P)\cup\{x_i\}=F_i.
\]
So \(x_i\) belongs to only \(1\) set of \(\mathcal F\), contradicting the condition that every element belongs to at least \(800\) sets.

Thus \(t\neq 1223\). Therefore
\[
t\le 1222.
\]

---

## Step 3: Construction showing \(1222\) is possible

Now we build a family where at least \(1222\) sets are needed.

Split \(X\) into two disjoint parts:
- \(C\) with \(|C|=800\),
- \(B\) with \(|B|=1222\).

For each \(b\in B\) and each \(c\in C\), define
\[
F_{b,c}=(C\setminus\{c\})\cup\{b\}.
\]
Each such set has size
\[
|C|-1+1=800.
\]

Let
\[
\mathcal F=\{F_{b,c}: b\in B,\ c\in C\}.
\]

### Check the conditions
- Every set has size \(800\).
- For \(b\in B\), the element \(b\) belongs to exactly \(800\) sets \(F_{b,c}\) (one for each \(c\in C\)).
- For \(c\in C\), the element \(c\) belongs to all sets except those with that same omitted \(c\), so it belongs to
  \[
  1222\cdot 799
  \]
  sets, certainly at least \(800\).

So all requirements are satisfied.

### Why at least \(1222\) sets are needed
Every set \(F_{b,c}\) contains exactly **one** element of \(B\). Therefore any union of \(m\) such sets can contain at most \(m\) elements of \(B\). Since \(|B|=1222\), to cover all of \(B\) we need at least \(1222\) sets.

### Why \(1222\) sets suffice
Pick one set for each \(b\in B\), choosing the omitted \(c\)'s so that not all \(800\) elements of \(C\) are omitted by every chosen set. For example, let the omissions cycle through the elements of \(C\). Then the union covers all of \(B\), and also all of \(C\). So \(1222\) sets do cover \(X\).

Hence this family has minimum cover size exactly \(1222\).

---

Since every such family can be covered by at most \(1222\) sets, and there exists a family requiring \(1222\) sets, the smallest guaranteed \(m\) is

\[
\boxed{1222}.
\]

Let  
\[
a_i=f(i)\qquad (1\le i\le 100).
\]
We want to minimize
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i
\]
under

1. \(|a_i-a_{i+1}|\le 1\) for \(1\le i\le 99\),
2. \(f(f(i))=100\) for all \(i\).

---

## Step 1: First note that \(f(100)=100\)

Since \(f(i)\in A\), every value \(f(i)\) is some integer in \(\{1,\dots,100\}\).  
Applying condition (2) to \(i=100\) is not enough directly, but observe:

If \(y\) is in the image of \(f\), say \(y=f(i)\), then
\[
f(y)=f(f(i))=100.
\]
So every image value maps to \(100\).

In particular, \(100\) itself is in the image (because \(f(y)=100\) for any image value \(y\)), hence
\[
f(100)=100.
\]

---

## Step 2: Let \(m=\min\{f(1),f(2),\dots,f(100)\}\)

Choose \(p\) such that \(f(p)=m\).

Since \(f(100)=100\) and adjacent values differ by at most \(1\), the sequence
\[
f(p),f(p+1),\dots,f(100)
\]
must pass through every integer from \(m\) up to \(100\).

So every integer
\[
m,m+1,\dots,100
\]
appears as a value of \(f\), i.e. is in the image of \(f\).

But every image value \(y\) satisfies \(f(y)=100\). Therefore
\[
f(m)=f(m+1)=\cdots=f(100)=100.
\]

So all positions \(m,m+1,\dots,100\) are forced to be \(100\).

---

## Step 3: Lower bound for the earlier values

Now look backward from position \(m\), where \(f(m)=100\).

Because adjacent differences are at most \(1\),

- \(f(m-1)\ge 99\),
- \(f(m-2)\ge 98\),
- ...
- in general,
\[
f(m-k)\ge 100-k.
\]

Also, by definition of \(m\), every value is at least \(m\). Hence
\[
f(m-k)\ge \max(m,100-k)\qquad (1\le k\le m-1).
\]

Thus, for fixed \(m\), the smallest possible values are exactly:
- \(f(i)=100\) for \(i\ge m\),
- going left, decrease by \(1\) each step until reaching \(m\), then stay at \(m\).

So the minimum sum for a given \(m\) is determined.

---

## Step 4: Compute the minimum sum for each \(m\)

### Case 1: \(m\le 50\)

Then before reaching index \(1\), the backward descent from \(100\) never gets as low as \(m\). So
\[
f(1),f(2),\dots,f(m-1)=101-m,102-m,\dots,99.
\]
Hence
\[
S(m)=100(101-m)+\sum_{t=101-m}^{99} t.
\]
This simplifies to
\[
S(m)=10000-\frac{m(m-1)}2.
\]
This decreases as \(m\) increases, so within this case the best is \(m=50\).

---

### Case 2: \(m\ge 51\)

Now the backward descent reaches \(m\) before index \(1\), so the minimal pattern is:

- first \(2m-100\) terms equal \(m\),
- then \(m+1,m+2,\dots,99\),
- then \(100\) from index \(m\) onward.

So
\[
S(m)=100(101-m)+m(2m-100)+\sum_{t=m+1}^{99} t.
\]
This simplifies to
\[
S(m)=15050+\frac{3m^2-401m}{2}.
\]

Now check the difference:
\[
S(m+1)-S(m)=3m-199.
\]
So \(S(m)\) decreases while \(3m-199<0\), i.e. \(m\le 66\), and increases for \(m\ge 67\).

Therefore the minimum occurs at
\[
m=67.
\]

---

## Step 5: Compute the minimum sum

For \(m=67\), the minimal sequence is

- \(f(i)=67\) for \(1\le i\le 34\),
- \(f(i)=i+33\) for \(35\le i\le 66\) (so these are \(68,69,\dots,99\)),
- \(f(i)=100\) for \(67\le i\le 100\).

Then
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} f(i)
=34\cdot 67+\sum_{t=68}^{99} t+34\cdot 100.
\]
Compute:
\[
34\cdot 67=2278,
\]
\[
\sum_{t=68}^{99} t=\frac{(68+99)\cdot 32}{2}=2672,
\]
\[
34\cdot 100=3400.
\]
So
\[
2278+2672+3400=8350.
\]

---

## Step 6: Verify the construction

Define
\[
f(i)=
\begin{cases}
67,&1\le i\le 34,\\
i+33,&35\le i\le 66,\\
100,&67\le i\le 100.
\end{cases}
\]

- Adjacent differences are \(0\) or \(1\), so condition (1) holds.
- The image is exactly \(\{67,68,\dots,100\}\).
- For every \(y\in\{67,\dots,100\}\), we have \(f(y)=100\).

Hence for every \(i\),
\[
f(f(i))=100.
\]
So the construction is valid.

Therefore the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{8350}.
\]

Let \(a_i=f(i)\) for \(1\le i\le 100\).  
Then the conditions become:

1. \(|a_i-a_{i+1}|\le 1\) for \(1\le i\le 99\),
2. \(a_{a_i}=100\) for all \(i\).

We want to minimize
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i.
\]

---

## Step 1: Let \(m\) be the minimum value of \(f\)

Set
\[
m=\min\{a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{100}\}.
\]
Choose \(p\) such that \(a_p=m\).

Since \(m\) is in the image of \(f\), condition (2) gives
\[
a_m=f(m)=100.
\]

So at position \(p\) the value is \(m\), and at position \(m\) the value is \(100\).

---

## Step 2: Show \(m\ge 51\)

From the Lipschitz condition,
\[
|a_m-a_p|\le |m-p|.
\]
Thus
\[
100-m \le |m-p|.
\]

If \(m\le 50\), then:

- if \(p<m\), then \(|m-p|\le m-1<100-m\),
- if \(p>m\), then \(p\le 99\), so \(|m-p|\le 99-m<100-m\).

Both are impossible. Hence
\[
m\ge 51.
\]

---

## Step 3: All integers \(m,m+1,\dots,100\) must map to \(100\)

Between positions \(p\) and \(m\), the values go from \(m\) to \(100\), changing by at most \(1\) each step. Since all values are integers, every value
\[
m,m+1,\dots,100
\]
must occur somewhere among \(a_1,\dots,a_{100}\).

But whenever a value \(k\) occurs, condition (2) says
\[
a_k=100.
\]
Therefore
\[
a_m=a_{m+1}=\cdots=a_{100}=100.
\]

So positions \(m,m+1,\dots,100\) are all forced to be \(100\).

---

## Step 4: Minimize the first \(m\) terms

For the first \(m\) positions, we know:

- each \(a_i\ge m\) (since \(m\) is the minimum),
- \(a_m=100\),
- adjacent differences are at most \(1\).

To make \(\sum_{i=1}^m a_i\) as small as possible, we should keep the values as low as possible for as long as possible, then increase by \(1\) each step until reaching \(100\) at position \(m\).

Thus the minimum possible pattern on the first \(m\) positions is:
\[
\underbrace{m,m,\dots,m}_{2m-100\text{ terms}},\; m+1,m+2,\dots,100.
\]
(Indeed, this uses \(2m-100\) copies of \(m\), then \(100-m\) increasing terms, for a total of \(m\) terms.)

So
\[
\sum_{i=1}^m a_i \ge m(2m-100)+\sum_{k=m+1}^{100} k.
\]

Also, for positions \(m+1\) to \(100\), all values are \(100\), so
\[
\sum_{i=m+1}^{100} a_i = 100(100-m).
\]

Hence
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i \ge m(2m-100)+\sum_{k=m+1}^{100} k+100(100-m).
\]

---

## Step 5: Simplify

Now
\[
\sum_{k=m+1}^{100} k=\frac{(m+1+100)(100-m)}{2}
=\frac{(101+m)(100-m)}{2}.
\]

Therefore
\[
S(m)=m(2m-100)+\frac{(101+m)(100-m)}{2}+100(100-m).
\]

Simplifying,
\[
S(m)=\frac{3m^2-401m+30100}{2}.
\]

This is a quadratic opening upward, so its minimum occurs near
\[
m=\frac{401}{6}\approx 66.83.
\]
Thus the minimizing integer is
\[
m=67.
\]

Compute:
\[
S(67)=\frac{3\cdot 67^2-401\cdot 67+30100}{2}
=\frac{13467-26867+30100}{2}
=\frac{16700}{2}
=8350.
\]

---

## Step 6: Construct a function achieving \(8350\)

Define
\[
f(i)=
\begin{cases}
67, & 1\le i\le 34,\\[2mm]
i+33, & 35\le i\le 67,\\[2mm]
100, & 68\le i\le 100.
\end{cases}
\]

So the sequence is
\[
67,67,\dots,67,68,69,\dots,100,100,\dots,100.
\]

Check:

- adjacent differences are \(0\) or \(1\),
- if \(i\le 34\), then \(f(i)=67\) and \(f(67)=100\),
- if \(35\le i\le 67\), then \(f(i)=i+33\in[68,100]\), and all those indices map to \(100\),
- if \(i\ge 68\), then \(f(i)=100\), so \(f(f(i))=f(100)=100\).

Thus all conditions hold.

Its sum is
\[
34\cdot 67 + (68+69+\cdots+100)+33\cdot 100
=2278+2772+3300=8350.
\]

So the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{8350}.
\]

Let \(a_i=f(i)\) for \(1\le i\le 100\). We want to minimize

\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i.
\]

## 1. Basic consequence of \(f(f(i))=100\)

If a value \(x\) appears among the \(a_i\)'s, say \(x=a_i\), then

\[
a_x=f(x)=f(f(i))=100.
\]

So:

- every value taken by the function is an index where the function value is \(100\).

In particular, since \(100=f(f(i))\) is a value of \(f\), we get

\[
a_{100}=100.
\]

---

## 2. Let \(s\) be the minimum value of \(f\)

Set

\[
s=\min_{1\le i\le 100} a_i.
\]

Choose \(k\) such that \(a_k=s\).

Because \(s\) is a value of \(f\), by the observation above,

\[
a_s=100.
\]

Now compare positions \(k\) and \(s\). Since adjacent values differ by at most \(1\),

\[
|a_s-a_k| = 100-s \le |s-k|.
\]

If \(k>s\), then \( |s-k|=k-s\), so

\[
100-s\le k-s \implies k\ge 100.
\]

Thus \(k=100\), but \(a_{100}=100\neq s\), contradiction unless \(s=100\). For the minimizing case \(s<100\), we must have \(k\le s\).

Hence

\[
100-s\le s-k\le s-1,
\]

so

\[
2s\ge 101 \implies s\ge 51.
\]

So the minimum value must satisfy

\[
s\in\{51,52,\dots,100\}.
\]

---

## 3. All numbers from \(s\) to \(100\) must map to \(100\)

Look at the segment from position \(k\) to position \(s\). We have

- \(a_k=s\),
- \(a_s=100\),
- adjacent differences are at most \(1\),
- and all values are \(\ge s\) because \(s\) is the minimum.

Therefore, as the sequence goes from \(s\) up to \(100\), it must pass through every integer

\[
s,s+1,\dots,100.
\]

Each of these appears as a value of \(f\), so each corresponding index maps to \(100\). Thus

\[
a_j=100\qquad \text{for all } j=s,s+1,\dots,100.
\]

---

## 4. Lower bound for the values before \(s\)

For \(i<s\):

- since every value of \(f\) must be one of \(s,s+1,\dots,100\), we have \(a_i\ge s\);
- also, since \(a_s=100\) and adjacent differences are at most \(1\),

\[
a_i\ge 100-(s-i)=100-s+i.
\]

So for \(i<s\),

\[
a_i\ge \max(s,\,100-s+i).
\]

Thus

\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i
\ge
\sum_{i=1}^{s-1}\max(s,100-s+i) + 100(101-s).
\]

For fixed \(s\), this lower bound is best possible.

---

## 5. Achieving the bound for a fixed \(s\)

Define

\[
a_i=
\begin{cases}
s, & 1\le i\le 2s-100,\\[2mm]
100-s+i, & 2s-99\le i\le s-1,\\[2mm]
100, & s\le i\le 100.
\end{cases}
\]

This sequence has adjacent differences \(0\) or \(1\), and its values are exactly in \(\{s,s+1,\dots,100\}\). Since every index \(j\ge s\) has \(a_j=100\), we get \(a_{a_i}=100\) for all \(i\). So this gives a valid function.

Therefore for each \(s\), the minimum possible sum is

\[
S(s)=s(2s-100)+\sum_{t=s+1}^{99} t+100(101-s).
\]

Compute:

\[
\sum_{t=s+1}^{99} t = 4950-\frac{s(s+1)}2.
\]

So

\[
S(s)=2s^2-100s+4950-\frac{s(s+1)}2+10100-100s
=\frac{3s^2-401s+30100}{2}.
\]

---

## 6. Minimize \(S(s)\) over \(51\le s\le 100\)

Consider the difference:

\[
S(s+1)-S(s)=3s-199.
\]

So:

- \(S(s+1)<S(s)\) when \(s\le 66\),
- \(S(s+1)>S(s)\) when \(s\ge 67\).

Hence the minimum occurs at

\[
s=67.
\]

Then

\[
S(67)=\frac{3\cdot 67^2-401\cdot 67+30100}{2}=8350.
\]

---

## 7. Example attaining the minimum

Take

\[
f(i)=
\begin{cases}
67, & 1\le i\le 34,\\
i+33, & 35\le i\le 66,\\
100, & 67\le i\le 100.
\end{cases}
\]

Then:

- adjacent differences are at most \(1\),
- values lie in \(\{67,68,\dots,100\}\),
- and every number in \(\{67,\dots,100\}\) maps to \(100\),

so \(f(f(i))=100\) for all \(i\).

Its sum is

\[
34\cdot 67 + (68+69+\cdots+99) + 34\cdot 100 = 8350.
\]

Therefore the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{8350}.
\]

Let \(a_i=f(i)\) for \(i=1,2,\dots,100\).  
We want to minimize
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i
\]
subject to

1. \(|a_i-a_{i+1}|\le 1\) for \(1\le i\le 99\),
2. \(f(f(i))=100\) for all \(i\).

---

### Step 1: Basic consequence of \(f(f(i))=100\)

If \(x\) is in the image of \(f\), say \(x=f(i)\), then
\[
f(x)=f(f(i))=100.
\]
So **every value attained by \(f\) must itself map to \(100\)**.

Also, since \(100=f(f(i))\), the value \(100\) is in the image of \(f\). Therefore
\[
f(100)=100.
\]
So
\[
a_{100}=100.
\]

---

### Step 2: Let \(m\) be the minimum value of \(f\)

Define
\[
m=\min\{a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{100}\}.
\]
Choose \(p\) such that \(a_p=m\).

Since \(a_{100}=100\) and adjacent terms differ by at most \(1\), the integer-valued sequence from \(a_p=m\) to \(a_{100}=100\) must pass through every integer
\[
m,m+1,\dots,100.
\]
Hence all of these numbers are attained as values of \(f\), so by the observation above,
\[
f(y)=100\qquad \text{for all } y=m,m+1,\dots,100.
\]
Thus
\[
a_m=a_{m+1}=\cdots=a_{100}=100.
\]

So the minimum \(m\) must occur at some index \(p<m\).

---

### Step 3: Lower bound on the earlier values

Since \(a_m=100\) and adjacent differences are at most \(1\), for any \(i<m\),
\[
a_i\ge 100-(m-i)=100-m+i.
\]
Also, since \(m\) is the minimum value,
\[
a_i\ge m.
\]
Therefore, for \(i<m\),
\[
a_i\ge \max(m,\,100-m+i).
\]

This forces the smallest possible shape:

- \(a_i=m\) for \(1\le i\le 2m-100\),
- then \(a_i=m+1,m+2,\dots,99\),
- then \(a_i=100\) for \(i=m,m+1,\dots,100\).

This only makes sense if \(2m-100\ge 1\), i.e.
\[
m\ge 51.
\]

---

### Step 4: Sum for a fixed \(m\)

So for a given \(m\ge 51\), the minimum possible sum is
\[
F(m)=m(2m-100)+\sum_{k=m+1}^{99}k+100(101-m).
\]

Compute:
\[
F(m)=\frac{3m^2-401m+30100}{2}.
\]

Now examine the difference:
\[
F(m+1)-F(m)=3m-199.
\]
So:

- \(F(m)\) decreases while \(3m-199<0\), i.e. \(m\le 66\),
- \(F(m)\) increases for \(m\ge 67\).

Hence the minimum occurs at
\[
m=67.
\]

Then
\[
F(67)=\frac{3(67)^2-401(67)+30100}{2}=8350.
\]

---

### Step 5: Construction attaining this value

Define
\[
f(i)=
\begin{cases}
67, & 1\le i\le 34,\\
i+33, & 35\le i\le 66,\\
100, & 67\le i\le 100.
\end{cases}
\]

Then:

- adjacent values differ by \(0\) or \(1\),
- the image is \(\{67,68,\dots,100\}\),
- for every \(y\in\{67,\dots,100\}\), we have \(f(y)=100\).

So indeed
\[
f(f(i))=100\quad \text{for all } i.
\]

Its sum is
\[
34\cdot 67+\sum_{k=68}^{99}k+34\cdot 100=8350.
\]

Therefore the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{8350}.
\]

Let \(a_i=f(i)\) for \(1\le i\le 100\).  
We want to minimize
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i
\]
subject to

1. \(|a_i-a_{i+1}|\le 1\) for \(1\le i\le 99\),
2. \(a_{a_i}=100\) for all \(i\).

---

## Step 1: Let \(m\) be the minimum value of \(f\)

Set
\[
m=\min\{a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{100}\}.
\]
Choose \(p\) such that \(a_p=m\).

Since \(a_{a_p}=a_m=100\), we have \(a_m=100\).

Now compare positions \(p\) and \(m\). Because adjacent values change by at most \(1\),
\[
|a_m-a_p|\le |m-p|.
\]
So
\[
100-m = |100-m| = |a_m-a_p|\le |m-p|.
\]

If \(m<100\), then \(p\neq 100\) (since \(a_{100}=100\), not \(m\)).  
Thus \(p< m\), so
\[
m-p\ge 100-m.
\]
Hence
\[
p\le 2m-100.
\]
Since \(p\ge 1\), we get
\[
2m-100\ge 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad m\ge 51.
\]

So the minimum value of \(f\) must satisfy
\[
m\ge 51.
\]

---

## Step 2: Show that \(f(j)=100\) for all \(j\ge m\)

We know \(a_p=m\) and \(a_m=100\), with \(p<m\).  
Going from position \(p\) to position \(m\), the values change by at most \(1\), starting at \(m\) and ending at \(100\). Therefore every integer
\[
m,m+1,\dots,100
\]
must appear somewhere among the values \(a_i\).

But whenever some value \(j\) appears, condition (2) says
\[
a_j=f(j)=100.
\]
So for every \(j\in\{m,m+1,\dots,100\}\),
\[
a_j=100.
\]

Thus
\[
a_m=a_{m+1}=\cdots=a_{100}=100.
\]

---

## Step 3: Lower bound the first \(m-1\) terms

For \(i<m\), two facts hold:

- Since \(m\) is the minimum value, \(a_i\ge m\).
- Since \(a_m=100\) and adjacent differences are at most \(1\),
  \[
  a_i\ge 100-(m-i)=i+100-m.
  \]

So for \(i<m\),
\[
a_i\ge \max\bigl(m,\; i+100-m\bigr).
\]

Now:

- If \(1\le i\le 2m-100\), then \(i+100-m\le m\), so \(a_i\ge m\).
- If \(2m-99\le i\le m-1\), then \(i+100-m>m\), so \(a_i\ge i+100-m\).

Therefore
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i
\ge m(2m-100)+\sum_{k=m+1}^{99} k +100(101-m).
\]

Compute:
\[
\sum_{k=m+1}^{99}k = 4950-\frac{m(m+1)}2.
\]
So
\[
S(m):=\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i
\ge m(2m-100)+4950-\frac{m(m+1)}2+100(101-m).
\]
This simplifies to
\[
S(m)\ge \frac{3m^2-401m+30100}{2}.
\]

---

## Step 4: Minimize this quadratic for integers \(m\ge 51\)

Consider
\[
\frac{3m^2-401m+30100}{2}.
\]
This is a convex quadratic, minimized near
\[
m=\frac{401}{6}\approx 66.83.
\]
So among integers, the minimum occurs at \(m=67\).

Then
\[
S(67)=\frac{3(67)^2-401(67)+30100}{2}
=\frac{13467-26867+30100}{2}
=\frac{16700}{2}=8350.
\]

So
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} f(i)\ge 8350.
\]

---

## Step 5: Construct a function achieving \(8350\)

Define
\[
f(i)=
\begin{cases}
67, & 1\le i\le 34,\\
i+33, & 35\le i\le 66,\\
100, & 67\le i\le 100.
\end{cases}
\]

So the sequence is
\[
67,67,\dots,67,68,69,\dots,99,100,100,\dots,100.
\]

### Check condition (1)
Adjacent differences are either \(0\) or \(1\), so \(|f(i)-f(i+1)|\le 1\).

### Check condition (2)
The values of \(f(i)\) are all in \(\{67,68,\dots,100\}\), and for every \(j\ge 67\),
\[
f(j)=100.
\]
Hence for every \(i\),
\[
f(f(i))=100.
\]

### Sum
\[
34\cdot 67+\sum_{k=68}^{99}k+34\cdot 100
=2278+2672+3400=8350.
\]

Thus the lower bound is attainable.

\[
\boxed{8350}
\]

Let  
\[
a_i=f(i)\qquad (1\le i\le 100).
\]
We want to minimize
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i.
\]

We are given:

1. \(|a_i-a_{i+1}|\le 1\) for \(1\le i\le 99\),
2. \(a_{a_i}=100\) for every \(i\).

---

## Step 1: Every value that appears must map to \(100\)

If \(y\) is a value of \(f\), then \(y=a_i\) for some \(i\). Hence
\[
a_y=f(y)=f(f(i))=100.
\]
So:

- every value appearing among \(a_1,\dots,a_{100}\) is an index at which the value is \(100\).

---

## Step 2: Let the minimum value be \(m\)

Set
\[
m=\min(a_1,\dots,a_{100}).
\]
Since \(m\) appears as a value, by Step 1 we get
\[
a_m=100.
\]

Now choose \(p\) such that \(a_p=m\).

Between positions \(p\) and \(m\), the sequence changes by at most \(1\) each step, while the endpoint values are \(m\) and \(100\). Therefore every integer
\[
m,m+1,\dots,100
\]
must appear somewhere in the sequence.

By Step 1 again, each such value \(t\) must satisfy \(a_t=100\). Hence
\[
a_m=a_{m+1}=\cdots=a_{100}=100.
\]

So all indices from \(m\) to \(100\) are forced to be \(100\).

---

## Step 3: This implies \(m\ge 51\)

Since \(a_p=m\), we cannot have \(p\ge m\), because all positions \(m,m+1,\dots,100\) already have value \(100\). Thus \(p<m\).

To go from value \(m\) at position \(p\) to value \(100\) at position \(m\), with adjacent changes at most \(1\), we need at least
\[
100-m
\]
steps. But only \(m-p\le m-1\) steps are available. So
\[
m-1\ge 100-m,
\]
which gives
\[
2m\ge 101 \quad\Rightarrow\quad m\ge 51.
\]

---

## Step 4: Lower bound for the sum when the minimum is \(m\)

We already know:
\[
a_i=100 \qquad (m\le i\le 100).
\]

For \(i<m\), since \(a_m=100\) and adjacent differences are at most \(1\),
\[
a_i\ge 100-(m-i)=100-m+i.
\]
Also \(a_i\ge m\) because \(m\) is the minimum.

Therefore
\[
a_i\ge \max(m,\,100-m+i)\qquad (1\le i<m).
\]

So
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i
\ge
\sum_{i=1}^{m-1}\max(m,100-m+i)+100(101-m).
\]

Because \(m\ge 51\), this lower bound becomes:

- \(a_i\ge m\) for \(i=1,\dots,2m-100\),
- \(a_i\ge 100-m+i\) for \(i=2m-99,\dots,m-1\).

Thus
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i
\ge
m(2m-100)+\sum_{k=m+1}^{99} k +100(101-m).
\]

Simplify:
\[
\sum_{k=m+1}^{99}k=\frac{99\cdot100}{2}-\frac{m(m+1)}2
=4950-\frac{m(m+1)}2.
\]
Hence
\[
S(m)\ge m(2m-100)+4950-\frac{m(m+1)}2+100(101-m).
\]
So
\[
S(m)\ge \frac{3m^2-401m+30100}{2}.
\]

---

## Step 5: Minimize this quadratic for \(m\ge 51\)

The quadratic
\[
\frac{3m^2-401m+30100}{2}
\]
is convex, and its vertex is at
\[
m=\frac{401}{6}\approx 66.83.
\]
So the minimum over integers occurs at \(m=67\).

Compute:
\[
S(67)=\frac{3(67)^2-401(67)+30100}{2}.
\]
Since
\[
67^2=4489,\quad 3\cdot4489=13467,\quad 401\cdot67=26867,
\]
we get
\[
S(67)=\frac{13467-26867+30100}{2}
=\frac{16700}{2}=8350.
\]

---

## Step 6: Show this value is attainable

Define \(f\) by
\[
f(i)=
\begin{cases}

i+33, & 35\le i\le 66,\\
100, & 67\le i\le 100.
\end{cases}
\]

So the sequence is
\[
67,67,\dots,67,68,69,\dots,99,100,100,\dots,100.
\]

Check:

- adjacent differences are \(0\) or \(1\), so condition (1) holds;
- every value of \(f(i)\) lies in \(\{67,68,\dots,100\}\), and for all \(j\in\{67,\dots,100\}\), \(f(j)=100\), so \(f(f(i))=100\) for every \(i\).

Its sum is
\[
34\cdot 67+\sum_{68}^{99}k+34\cdot 100.
\]
Now
\[
34\cdot 67=2278,\qquad \sum_{68}^{99}k=\frac{(68+99)\cdot 32}{2}=2672,\qquad 34\cdot100=3400.
\]
Thus
\[
2278+2672+3400=8350.
\]

So the minimum possible sum is indeed

\[
\boxed{8350}.
\]

Let  
\[
a_i=f(i)\qquad (1\le i\le 100).
\]
Then the conditions become:

1. \(|a_i-a_{i+1}|\le 1\) for \(1\le i\le 99\);
2. \(a_{a_i}=100\) for every \(i\).

We want to minimize
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i.
\]

---

## Step 1: Understand what values can appear

Let
\[
m=\min\{a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{100}\}.
\]
Choose \(p\) such that \(a_p=m\).

Since \(a_{a_p}=a_m=100\), and consecutive terms differ by at most \(1\), we have the global Lipschitz bound
\[
|a_p-a_m|\le |p-m|.
\]
So
\[
100-m=|m-100|=|a_p-a_m|\le |p-m|.
\]
If \(p>m\), then this would give
\[
100-m\le p-m \implies 100\le p,
\]
so \(p=100\), but then \(a_{100}=m\), impossible unless \(m=100\). That is not minimal. Hence \(p<m\).

Now from position \(p\) to position \(m\), the sequence goes from value \(m\) to value \(100\) with step size at most \(1\). Therefore it must hit every integer
\[
m,m+1,\dots,100.
\]
So all these numbers appear as values of \(f\). Hence the image of \(f\) is exactly
\[
\{m,m+1,\dots,100\}.
\]

---

## Step 2: What happens at indices \(m,m+1,\dots,100\)?

If \(t\) is in the image of \(f\), then \(a_t=f(t)=100\) because \(a_{a_i}=100\) for all \(i\).

Since the image is \(\{m,m+1,\dots,100\}\), we get
\[
a_t=100 \qquad \text{for all } t=m,m+1,\dots,100.
\]

So the last \(101-m\) positions \(m,m+1,\dots,100\) are all equal to \(100\).

---

## Step 3: Minimize the earlier values

Now consider \(j<m\).

- Because all values of \(f\) lie in the image, we must have
  \[
  a_j\ge m.
  \]
- Also, since \(a_m=100\) and \(|a_j-a_m|\le m-j\),
  \[
  a_j \ge 100-(m-j)=j+100-m.
  \]

Therefore
\[
a_j\ge \max(m,\;j+100-m).
\]

To minimize the sum, we should take equality everywhere:
\[
a_j=\max(m,\;j+100-m)\qquad (j<m).
\]

So the minimal-shape sequence for a fixed \(m\) is
\[
\underbrace{m,\dots,m}_{2m-100\text{ times}},\; m+1,m+2,\dots,99,\;
\underbrace{100,\dots,100}_{101-m\text{ times}}.
\]

For this to actually contain the value \(m\), we need \(2m-100\ge 1\), so
\[
m\ge 51.
\]

---

## Step 4: Sum for a fixed \(m\)

Thus
\[
S(m)=m(2m-100)+\sum_{t=m+1}^{99} t +100(101-m).
\]

Compute:
\[
\sum_{t=m+1}^{99} t=\frac{(m+1+99)(99-m)}{2}
=\frac{(m+100)(99-m)}{2}.
\]

Hence
\[
S(m)=m(2m-100)+\frac{(m+100)(99-m)}{2}+100(101-m).
\]

This simplifies to
\[
S(m)=\frac{3m^2-401m+30100}{2}.
\]

Now check where this is minimal for integers \(m\in[51,100]\). Use differences:
\[
S(m+1)-S(m)=3m-199.
\]
So:

- \(S(m+1)<S(m)\) when \(m\le 66\),
- \(S(m+1)>S(m)\) when \(m\ge 67\).

Therefore the minimum occurs at
\[
m=67.
\]

---

1. \(|a_i-a_{i+1}|\le 1\) for \(1\le i\le 99\),
2. \(a_{a_i}=100\) for all \(i\).

We want to minimize
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i.
\]

---

## Step 1: Let the minimum value be \(m\)

Set
\[
m=\min\{a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{100}\}.
\]
Choose an index \(p\) such that \(a_p=m\).

Since \(a_{a_p}=100\), we get
\[
a_m=100.
\]

Also, from \(a_m=100\), condition (2) gives
\[
a_{100}=100.

## Step 2: Show that \(a_j=100\) for every \(j=m,m+1,\dots,100\)

Between positions \(p\) and \(m\), the sequence goes from value \(m\) to value \(100\), changing by at most \(1\) each step. Hence every integer
\[
m,m+1,\dots,100

must appear somewhere among the values \(a_i\).

a_j=a_{a_q}=100.

Therefore,
\[
a_j=100\qquad \text{for all }j=m,m+1,\dots,100.

So positions \(m,m+1,\dots,100\) all have value \(100\).

---

## Step 3: Deduce \(m\ge 51\)

Since \(a_j=100\) for all \(j\ge m\), the minimum value \(m\) must occur at some position \(p<m\).

Now \(a_p=m\) and \(a_m=100\), so by the adjacent-step condition,
\[
100-m\le m-p\le m-1.

Thus
\[
100-m\le m-1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
2m\ge 101
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
m\ge 51.

---

## Step 4: Lower bound for the sequence when \(m\) is fixed

For \(i<m\), since \(a_m=100\) and adjacent differences are at most \(1\),
\[

a_i\ge \max(m,\ i+100-m).

And for \(i\ge m\),
\[
a_i=100.

\[
S(m)=\sum_{i=1}^{m-1}\max(m,\ i+100-m)+100(101-m).

\[

so
\[
S(m)=2m^2-100m+4950-\frac{m(m+1)}2+10100-100m
=\frac{3m^2-401m+30100}{2}.
\]

\[
m=\frac{401}{6}\approx 66.83,
\]
so the minimizing integer is \(m=67\).

\[
S(67)=\frac{3(67)^2-401(67)+30100}{2}
=\frac{13467-26867+30100}{2}

---

Define
\[
a_i=

100, & 67\le i\le 100.

So the sequence is
\[
67,67,\dots,67,68,69,\dots,99,100,100,\dots,100.

- Adjacent terms differ by at most \(1\).
- The values taken are exactly \(67,68,\dots,100\).
- For every \(j\in\{67,\dots,100\}\), we have \(a_j=100\).

Thus for every \(i\),
\[
a_{a_i}=100,
\]
so all conditions are satisfied.

Its sum is
\[
34\cdot 67 + (68+69+\cdots+99) + 34\cdot 100 = 8350.

\[
\boxed{8350}.
\]

Let  
\[
y_k=\sqrt{x_k^2-x_{k-1}^2}\qquad (k=1,\dots,n),
\]
so that \(y_k>0\) and
\[
x_k=\sqrt{x_{k-1}^2+y_k^2}.
\]

We seek the smallest \(a\) such that
\[
a\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{(k+1)^{3/2}}{y_k}\ge \sum_{k=1}^n \frac{k^2+3k+3}{x_k}
\]
for all \(n\) and all admissible \(x_0,\dots,x_n\).

---

## 1) A useful 2-term lemma

For positive numbers \(U,V,s,t\),
\[
\frac{U}{s}+\frac{V}{t}\ge \frac{(U^{2/3}+V^{2/3})^{3/2}}{\sqrt{s^2+t^2}}.
\tag{1}
\]
This is the minimum of \(\frac Us+\frac Vt\) under \(s^2+t^2=\text{const}\), found by Lagrange multipliers. Equality holds iff
\[
s:t=U^{1/3}:V^{1/3}.
\tag{2}
\]

---

## 2) Guess the right constant

We will prove that
\[
a_0=\frac{16\sqrt2}{9}
\]
works, and is best possible.

In fact, we prove the stronger statement
\[
a_0\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{(k+1)^{3/2}}{y_k}
\ge
\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{k^2+3k+3}{x_k}+\frac{n^3}{9x_n}.
\tag{3}
\]

Since the extra term is positive, this implies the desired inequality.

---

## 3) Proof of (3) by induction

### Base case \(n=1\)

Since \(y_1=x_1\),
\[
a_0\frac{2\sqrt2}{x_1}
=\frac{16\sqrt2}{9}\cdot \frac{2\sqrt2}{x_1}
=\frac{64}{9x_1}.
\]
And
\[
\frac{1^2+3\cdot1+3}{x_1}+\frac{1^3}{9x_1}
=\frac{7}{x_1}+\frac{1}{9x_1}
=\frac{64}{9x_1}.
\]
So equality holds.

---

### Inductive step

Assume (3) holds for \(n-1\). Then
\[
a_0\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\frac{(k+1)^{3/2}}{y_k}
\ge
\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\frac{k^2+3k+3}{x_k}+\frac{(n-1)^3}{9x_{n-1}}.
\]
Adding the \(k=n\) term gives
\[
a_0\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{(k+1)^{3/2}}{y_k}
\ge
\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\frac{k^2+3k+3}{x_k}
+
\left(
\frac{(n-1)^3}{9x_{n-1}}
+
a_0\frac{(n+1)^{3/2}}{y_n}
\right).
\]
Now apply lemma (1) to
\[
U=\frac{(n-1)^3}{9},\qquad V=a_0 (n+1)^{3/2},\qquad s=x_{n-1},\qquad t=y_n,
\]
noting that \(\sqrt{s^2+t^2}=x_n\). Then
\[
\frac{(n-1)^3}{9x_{n-1}}+a_0\frac{(n+1)^{3/2}}{y_n}
\ge
\frac{\left(\left(\frac{(n-1)^3}{9}\right)^{2/3}+\left(a_0(n+1)^{3/2}\right)^{2/3}\right)^{3/2}}{x_n}.
\]
Now
\[
\left(\frac{(n-1)^3}{9}\right)^{2/3}=\frac{(n-1)^2}{9^{2/3}},
\]
and since \(a_0=\frac{16\sqrt2}{9}\),
\[
a_0^{2/3}=\left(\frac{16\sqrt2}{9}\right)^{2/3}=\frac{8}{9^{2/3}}.
\]
So
\[
\left(a_0(n+1)^{3/2}\right)^{2/3}
=
\frac{8(n+1)}{9^{2/3}}.
\]
Hence
\[
\left(\frac{(n-1)^2+8(n+1)}{9^{2/3}}\right)^{3/2}
=
\left(\frac{(n+3)^2}{9^{2/3}}\right)^{3/2}
=
\frac{(n+3)^3}{9}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\frac{(n-1)^3}{9x_{n-1}}+a_0\frac{(n+1)^{3/2}}{y_n}
\ge
\frac{(n+3)^3}{9x_n}.
\]
But
\[
\frac{(n+3)^3}{9}
=
\frac{n^3}{9}+n^2+3n+3.
\]
So
\[
\frac{(n+3)^3}{9x_n}
=
\frac{n^2+3n+3}{x_n}+\frac{n^3}{9x_n}.
\]
Substitute back:
\[
a_0\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{(k+1)^{3/2}}{y_k}
\ge
\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{k^2+3k+3}{x_k}
+
\frac{n^3}{9x_n}.
\]
Thus (3) holds for \(n\). Induction is complete.

So \(a_0=\frac{16\sqrt2}{9}\) is sufficient.

---

## 4) Sharpness

Now we show no smaller constant works.

Take
\[
x_k=\frac{k(k+1)(k+2)(k+3)}{24}\qquad (k\ge1).
\]
Then \(0=x_0<x_1<x_2<\cdots\), and
\[
\frac{x_{k-1}}{x_k}=\frac{k-1}{k+3}.
\]
Hence
\[
y_k=\sqrt{x_k^2-x_{k-1}^2}
=
x_k\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{k-1}{k+3}\right)^2}
=
x_k\cdot \frac{2\sqrt{2(k+1)}}{k+3}.
\]
This is exactly the equality condition (2) in each induction step above, and the base case also has equality. Therefore for this choice of \(x_k\),
\[
a_0\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{(k+1)^{3/2}}{y_k}
=
\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{k^2+3k+3}{x_k}
+\frac{n^3}{9x_n}.
\tag{4}
\]
Now
\[
x_n=\frac{n(n+1)(n+2)(n+3)}{24}\sim \frac{n^4}{24},
\]
so
\[
\frac{n^3}{9x_n}\to 0.
\]
Also
\[
\frac{(k+1)^{3/2}}{y_k}
=
\frac{6\sqrt2}{k(k+2)},
\]
so the denominator sum stays bounded away from \(0\). Thus from (4),
\[
\frac{\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{k^2+3k+3}{x_k}}
{\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{(k+1)^{3/2}}{y_k}}
\to a_0.
\]
Therefore any \(a<a_0\) fails for sufficiently large \(n\). So \(a_0\) is the minimum possible.

---

\[
\boxed{\frac{16\sqrt2}{9}}
\]

Let  
\[
\Delta_k=x_k^2-x_{k-1}^2>0 \qquad (k=1,\dots,n).
\]
Then
\[
x_k^2=\Delta_1+\cdots+\Delta_k.
\]

We want the least \(a\) such that
\[
